titleStmt.title: Introduction to the Site of Mount Culai

div.head: 徂徠山

figure.head: Mount Culai, Radiant Buddha Cliff (Yingfoyan 映佛巖) as seen from Guanghua Monastery (Guanghua Si 光化寺); photograph taken in 2007
由徂徠山光化寺眺望映佛巖。2007年拍攝

div.p: 徂徠山與其他刻經山相距甚遠。該山有兩處刻經址，一處位於峰頂，另一處在山谷，鄰近一座寺院。峰頂的主要刻經是山東地區常見的關於佛性的九十八字節文，這段文字適於禪修。經主王子椿是一位支持當地僧團的官員。山谷刻經則列出了菩薩道各階位不同形態的“空”。除此處之外，山東地區僅存一例。此處還刻有四則佛名，代表無限的時間與空間，保證佛性來世永存。此處經主也是王子椿，但由他與其家族成員共同施造。
徂徠山刻經的研究史引人注目。南宋紹熙三年（1192）已有著録，數百年後，金石學家纔注意到山東地區其他刻經。對徂徠山刻經的全面系統研究始於18世紀。其中，當地學者聶鈫（字劍光，1711－1796）卓立超群，是最早研究刻經文本的金石學家之一。

div.head: 地理位置
div.p: 徂徠山爲魯中地區的獨立山脈。北面的大汶河往西南偏西流入東平湖，將徂徠山與泰山山脈分隔。南面的山谷有柴汶河向西流，於大汶口鎮附近流入大汶河。徂徠山屬新泰市轄區（見封二地圖）。
映佛巖位於徂徠山脈東南角低矮的峰頂，氣勢宏偉，存有四則刻銘（徂徠山1號至4號；）。其地理位置是東經117.3938度，北緯36.0112度。相當於UTM坐標系統第50S帶，東行535490，北行3985260。
映佛巖西邊山谷約一公里處有一座現代寺院，位於古代光化寺舊址（）。一塊等人高的大圓石（）立於通往寺院的路旁，距離映佛巖0.7公里（）。此石地理位置是東經117.3882度，北緯36.0153度，相當於UTM坐標系統第50S帶，東行534980，北行3985720。此圓石上有六則刻銘（徂徠山5號至10號）。
徂徠山這兩處刻經址是山東省刻經地處最偏遠的。距其最近的是西北34公里處的泰山經石峪《金剛經》和西南45公里處的鳳凰山佛名。從徂徠山的刻經址遠眺，無法看到這些石刻，因爲西面與南面的山峰阻擋了視綫。映佛巖刻銘面朝西南偏南，光化寺附近圓石主面的刻銘（徂徠山5至7號）則朝向西南方。如同徂徠山一樣，泰山、鳳凰山及水牛山的刻銘都朝向南方。
徂徠山地處偏遠，但若沿着曠谷行走，或可望見東平和鄒城的刻經群。徂徠山向西約100公里是東平湖，關於東平湖周圍的刻銘，見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷。往南同等距離即鄒城。關於鄒城周圍的刻銘，見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷。經由鳳凰山再轉向南方約40公里，可經曲阜抵達鄒城。

div.head: 地形與空間構成
div.p: 徂徠山兩處刻經的年代爲北齊。一處刻於映佛巖立石，高近4米，金石學家稱其“白石如屏”。其上刻《文殊師利所説摩訶般若波羅蜜經》九十八字節文（徂徠山1號）。《文殊師利所説摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#232, 8: 731a15－21和《文殊師利所説般若波羅蜜經》T#233, 8: 738a19－24。 經文左側上方的岩石部分斷裂，形成可容一人或站或坐的坡道。其上有一塊平滑且垂直的石面，上刻“般若波羅蜜”（徂徠山2號）及兩則題記（徂徠山3和4號；和）。
映佛巖前有一塊寬敞而平坦的岩石，彷彿邀請來訪者面對刻經席地而坐（和）。此石可能是禪修静坐之所。類似的石座也見於洪頂山（洪頂山16.2號前）。《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第9－11頁和第76－77頁，圖12－13。嶧山妖精洞（嶧山3號）也可能是禪修之處。《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷第75頁，圖25。此三處所刻經文均爲同一段九十八字節文。

figure.head: The southeasternmost range of Mount Culai, with Mount Xie, and Radiant Buddha Cliff at the summit of a small hill in the foreground, seen from the south; photograph taken in 2007
自南觀望徂徠山山脈東南角和薤山，映佛巖位於前部小山丘頂峰。2007年拍攝

div.p: 徂徠山的第二處刻經址在山谷。爲了加以保護，經石現以鐵籠屋圍住（）。經石南面刊刻一段列有“十七空”的經文（徂徠山5號），緊接其後的是一則經主題記（徂徠山6號）。另一位供養人的名字位於經文下方（徂徠山7號；）。經石東面一處面積較小的平面上刻有另一則題記（徂徠山8號；）。據稱，其左側即失佚的四則佛名（徂徠山9號和10號）。

div.head: 地名
div.p: 《詩經·魯頌·閟宫》首次提及徂徠山脈。“徂來之松，新甫之柏。是斷是度，是尋是尺。”《詩經今注》第519頁。唐劉禹錫（772－842）撰《崔公神道碑》亦沿用此名。“善積家肥，子孫多材，如彼榱棟，必生徂徠。”《劉禹錫集箋證》第84頁。英譯見韋利第272頁。 徂徠山脈占地173平方公里，有99座大小不同的山峰。位於中央的主峰太平頂海拔高度達1027米。《徂徠山誌》第33頁。其東南7公里處爲寨山，數種金石文獻提及此山，並標出薤山的相對地理位置。文獻稱，薤山在寨山之東，山中有光化寺及映佛巖。《泰山道里記》第66頁：“……爲寨山。又東五里爲薤山，藍溪出焉。水東南流過鎮里村西，又南入小汶。其上有光化禪寺。”然而，金石文獻所記寨山和薤山之間的距離不盡相同。例如，唐仲冕（1753－1827）《岱覽》第4册，卷二十二，第480頁：“薤山在寨山東八里。”這些文獻亦記載薤山上稱爲圓瓦崖的巖壁，據説此巖壁在薤山東北面。原大悲庵位於圓瓦崖，照片見《徂徠山誌》第253頁。其殘垣今在新建光化寺正殿旁。從可清楚看出圓瓦崖的形狀，圓瓦崖位於徂徠山5到10號刻銘的右側。金石學家聶鈫、宋思仁（1730－1807）、段松苓（1745－1800）、阮元（1764－1849）、王昶（1725－1806）和法偉堂（1843－1907）等提及大悲庵，並稱映佛巖在其東南方二三里處。

div.head: 相關寺院
div.p: 光化寺在徂徠山東南麓，據稱建於北魏（386－534），北宋祥符間（1008－1016）賜號“崇慶”。寺内《重修光化禪寺之記》碑立於1246年，至今猶存。從碑額“禪”字，可看出此寺宋時爲禪宗寺院，碑曰：
div.quote: 徂徠光化寺者，其來遠矣，始創迹於後魏，至隋朝而有光化之名。唐有天下，三百餘年，衣缽相傳，宗派不泯。繼以五代之亂，寺從而廢。趙宋開國，迨及祥符七年，復賜號“崇慶”，而與明孔山之靈巖相爲甲乙。無何貞祐之變，指1213年蒙古人入侵。殿宇堂廡盡爲灰燼，靡有孑遺。嗚呼！《泰山誌》第7册：第510－511頁。
div.p: 光化寺於元初時重修，如今，寺院基址大部分爲徂徠山林場宿舍所覆蓋。因尚未展開考古勘探，寺院原始規模和佈局不得而知。由李昉（925－996）等人編纂於978年的《太平廣記》卷四一七收有《集異記》，其中的“光化寺客”描述了光化寺的景况，雖出小説家言，但也可作爲了解光化寺的間接資料。《太平廣記》第9册：第3394－3395頁。

1983年，寺址東南方半公里處出土佛像8件，其中，名爲羊銀光的女供養人捐造的一鋪石造像保存最好，今存新泰市博物館。穆紅梅和馬培林。該像高60釐米，正面刻一佛二菩薩像，背面刻銘文四行三十二字：“□□□和三年四月壬寅朔八日己酉，傳統上視此日爲佛誕日。清信女佛弟子羊銀光造像一軀，所願從心。”銘文首行前三字殘泐，根據風格判斷，造像係東魏（534－550）之作，殘字應爲“大魏興”三字。羊銀光造像的出土，可證《重修光化禪寺之記》所載光化寺創建年代是有根據的。

div.head: 相關人物
div.p: 北朝時期，徂徠山屬梁父縣。徂徠山刻經最重要的供養人是梁父縣縣令王子椿。徂徠山3號及6號題記有其名。映佛巖的石刻由一個邑社組織通力完成，一則題記（徂徠山4號）記録了此邑社的主要成員。相較之下，光化寺附近大圓石上的刻經主要由一個家族所施造，即王子椿與其五位子女共同供養。此圓石上還可見另一位地方官的名字，即一位名爲胡賓的中正（徂徠山8號），王子椿應當認識此人。見下文“題記”部分。

王子椿事迹失載，但他或許也曾在其他地區資助佛事。吳式芬（1796－1856）《攈古録》提到費縣興聖寺造像碑，該碑由一名爲王子□（此字殘缺）等四十人造，日期爲武平三年三月八日（572年4月6日）。“興聖寺王子□等四十人造四面石碑像，正書。額題‘興聖寺主’，諸葛始興隸書。紀年一行，篆書。山東費縣。武平三年三月八日。”《攈古録》第34頁。費縣位於徂徠山東南方約一百公里處，所以，此造像碑的供養人“王子□”可能也是王子椿。

div.head: 斷代
div.p: 映佛巖王子椿題記（徂徠山4號）與光化寺附近的胡賓題記（徂徠山8號）紀年相同，均爲武平元年（570）。且兩則題記書風相近。

figure.head: The inscribed boulder at Radiant Buddha Cliff; photograph taken in 2007
映佛巖刻石。2007年拍攝

div.head: 研究史
div.p: 早在金代（1125－1234），徂徠山的佛教銘刻已爲當時學界所知。18世紀以降，著録徂徠山刻經的文獻主要有遊記、有關遊記類文體發展的簡述，見梅綺雯。何瞻的研究更爲詳盡，並討論此文體的複雜性。地方誌或山誌根據卜正民第49頁，山誌隸屬地方誌。山誌的形成，見第11－86頁。地域認同的驕傲推動了地方誌的編纂，杭州地方誌的編纂即爲一例，見莫克莉第233頁。及金石學著作等三類。 有關金石學史的綜述，見趙超第118－167頁，以及宗小娜。一般而言，後者是主要研究文獻。一位並不廣爲人知的當地學者聶鈫 聶氏之名“鈫”常被誤作“鈙”或“敘”。其生卒年，見車錫倫。根據實地踏訪，撰寫了一部涉及徂徠山銘刻的遊記。關於徂徠山的最重要的金石筆記，出自博學的錢大昕（1728－1804）。這兩位學者蒐集了刻經址和銘文的大部分資料。至20世紀，嚴謹的日本學者首次以攝影技術，對刻經址作了完整的記録。此處刻經的研究史長達千年，爲山東金石學研究的典範。

徂徠山刻經的最早記録見於南宋紹熙三年（1192）安升卿的一則刻銘。可見在地方史愈見重視之時，該址已爲人所知。有關宋元時期地方誌形成期，以婺州爲例，見包弼德之討論。該題記最初以楷書刻於名爲“屏風岩”的石上，今已佚失。《徂徠山誌》第240頁指出一屏風形的岩石上有“屏風石”題刻，位於今十六峪景區，距離光化寺西側數里處。據稱此題刻字徑約70釐米。然而，我們並不清楚這是否爲刻有安升卿的題記的“屏風岩”。唐仲冕《岱覽》第4册，卷二十二，第475頁徂徠山附圖的背景部分載有屏風岩，相對位置在光化寺的左側。宋代以降，注重歷史和古物的學者便在鄉間尋訪題刻，並且取得拓本以爲戰利品。這個稱爲“訪古碑”的活動於明清時期漸趨風行。“訪古碑”的早期階段，見鲁道夫第173頁。明清階段，見陳雲倩第49頁。有時學者們也留下題刻記述他們的行迹，有關宋代文人參訪刻經址的活動，見張聰第154－179頁。安升卿便是如此。他簡要地記述了和友人“游徂徠探古迹”，並且“得冠軍石經於佛谷”。“安升卿題名……游徂徠探古迹，……得冠軍石經於佛谷，……明昌壬子孟夏記。”《岱覽》第4册，卷二十二，第490頁。這位“冠軍”基本上可確定是王子椿，他的名字見於兩則題記，即徂徠山3號和徂徠山5號。由於其他資料未提及“佛谷”這個地名，因此難以判定安氏看到的是哪段刻經。
18世紀，學者詳盡評述並著録徂徠山刻經。這段時期的著作主要有上文提及的遊記、地方誌及金石文獻三類。遊記提及刻經並對刻經址定位，提供距離和地貌資訊。金石文獻採用目録的方式，提供按次序編排的文字材料。地方誌介於兩者之間，既提供地貌資訊，也提供目録式條目。每部著作的用意和框架不同，刻經資料的形式和編排卻很類似，即内容、斷代、書法風格、地貌、地理位置和所屬轄區等。
這三類文獻提供研究石刻文獻的思想、社會政治和技術背景的框架。但只有作者個人的活動與交遊纔能讓我們理解當初刻經時的歷史背景脈絡。

例如，借助印刷術之便，地方誌早在宋代已經問世。至16世紀，印刷成本低廉，地方誌大量出現，且卷帙繁多。有關早期資助刊印方誌的問題，見戴思哲第124－128頁，晚清時期的情况，見戴思哲第213－248頁。此時出版了最初幾部有關泰山地區的山誌，徂徠山也包含其中。但不論明嘉靖三十四年（1555）刊刻的汪子卿《泰山誌》，還是明萬曆十五年（1587）刊刻查志隆《岱史》，《岱史》。這兩部私人著述均未提及徂徠山刻經。直到清乾隆時期，朝廷命官發起調查，召集地方博學之士參與，刻經資料纔得以著録，並付諸棗梨。
其中最出色的學者當屬聶鈫。我們對聶氏所知不多，只知他成長於泰山山麓，以通曉地方文化聞名。由下文可知，他的著作可證鄉土資料之於如地方誌中金石部分的編纂何其重要。清乾隆八年（1743），聶鈫陪同官員劉履夫（活動於1743－1748年），考察御道古跡。劉氏隨後編纂《泰山紀略》，並把書稿交與聶鈫。 《泰山道里記》第23頁。

清乾隆二十五年（1760），成城（1713－？）根據《泰山紀略》的資料修纂《泰安府誌》。有關其編修過程，見戴思哲第145－152頁。其中“金石誌”部分記述了徂徠山三則刻銘。首先是“北齊《大般若經》石刻”（徂徠山5號、6號和8號），刻於光化寺附近巨石；再者是“北齊佛名石刻”（徂徠山9號和10號），刻於第一組刻經旁；其次是“北齊《般若波羅蜜經》石刻”（徂徠山2號、3號、1號和4號，此從成城排列順序），位於映佛巖。成城未著録佛名及徂徠山1號佛經節文，稱其風化嚴重。 《泰安府誌》第229頁。

朱孝純（1762年舉人）的《泰山圖誌》（1774年序）是第一部提及徂徠山刻經的山誌。此書著於朱氏任泰安知府期間，旨在爲泰山舊誌查漏補闕。朱氏出於地方自豪感，其書鉅細靡遺，不少史料由此得以保存，不致湮滅，或爲天災、蠹蟲所毀。 《泰山圖誌》第3頁。此書詳細記載了泰山山脈及其地標，但石刻僅録經名。卷五下“金石”篇有附録“徂徠諸山金石及碎刻”。此處列出三組銘刻的三個條目，一如上述地方誌所載。他似乎得知聶鈫即將補正刊刻上述資料。

泰山的地方誌迄今所見卷帙最多的當屬唐仲冕（1753－1827）《岱覽》，其中卷二十二載徂徠山風物。唐仲冕賜進士，但並未做官。此書撰寫於他執掌泰山書院期間，始於1782年，成書於1793年。不同於朱孝純，他未將徂徠山的地貌和銘刻分開描述，而是以其與周圍標誌性景物的相對距離展開。唐仲冕不僅與成城同樣提到四佛名（徂徠山9號和10號），還轉録佛名。他是首次將其書法辨識爲八分書的金石學者。他還指出武平元年乃天統六年，也就是庚寅年（570）。此二者皆爲北齊後主高緯（557－577年在位）年號。據正史，天統年號僅用了5年。
聶鈫不滿舊有的泰山地誌，尤其是成城《泰安府誌》的某些謬誤和疏漏，在清乾隆三十七年（1772）《泰山道里記》序舉出其錯誤及遺漏。這些錯誤促使他實地考察，正如此序所云： 近乃豎梯，親加刮摩以核之。雖風雨寒暑，弗憚其勞。而又質之野老，參考群書，竭半生精力。 《泰山道里記》第23頁。

清乾隆三十至三十七年（1765－1772），聶氏四易其稿，始成定本，卻覺言猶未盡。讀者讚揚其叙事，譽爲“以絡爲綱”聶學文（1760年舉人）1766年序，《泰山道里記》第75頁。。聶鈫“以山之脈絡爲文之脈絡”徐宗幹（1796－1866）1826年序，《泰山道里記》第24頁。引導讀者，有親臨此山之感： 或即藉是以當臥游，則又不異與劍光芒鞋竹杖，登陟五千仞間也。高怡（活動於18世紀下半葉）1773年序，《泰山道里記》第28頁。其他寺誌所提之“臥游”，見馬德偉第54頁。其歷史背景見王燕寧第3－7頁。
從《泰山道里記》八篇序文可看出此書對後來學者的影響，其中一篇由錢大昕所作（見下文）。這本文化之旅構成的遊記令人愛不釋手，成爲遊客導覽和學者考古的基本用書。

聶鈫躬身考察徂徠山石刻，其研究在三方面超越了地方誌。其一，轉録徂徠山1號刻銘的經文；其二，改正程志隆《泰山縣誌》誤讀的胡賓題記（徂徠山8號）首二字，該縣誌與《泰安府誌》同修於乾隆三十一年（1760）；其三，增補映佛巖普憘等題記（徂徠山4號）開頭“普憘”二字，並辨識第四行的四字，比之前多了一個字。這些刻字的釋讀一直存有争議，其中有些人贊同聶鈫的看法，或許因他是早期見過實物的可信任的目擊者，但仍有其他學者因證據不足，未下結論。
聶鈫在學術上的主要貢獻是指出映佛巖《文殊師利所説摩訶般若波羅蜜經》（徂徠山1號）與泰山經石峪《金剛經》（泰山1號）書風相似。其侄聶學文（1760年舉人）乾隆三十五年（1766）於《泰山道里記》跋中也特别强調這一點。 《泰山道里記》第75頁。

聶鈫引用學者王世懋（1536－1588）一個世紀前的看法，王世懋生卒和簡介，見。王世懋質疑《金剛經》刻石歸於王羲之（303－361）名下的普遍説法，並認爲此經應刻於宋或元。然而，聶鈫對王世懋的推測存疑。見下文徂徠山1號“討論”：《潛研堂金石文跋尾》第18767頁。因經主王子椿活動於6世紀，徂徠山刻經節文（徂徠山1號）紀年明確。通過風格分析，聶鈫認爲，金剛經銘刻與徂徠山刻經的隸書出自同一人，即王子椿。其後，錢大昕對此看法深表贊同。陳雲倩第38頁研究了另一種書風。她先徵引都穆（1459－1525）《金薤琳琅》。都穆認可鄭樵（1104－1162）的看法，把《豳州昭仁寺碑》歸爲虞世南（558－638）的書作，但他未顧及此作與虞世南公認的《孔子廟堂碑》書風不同之處。陳雲倩認爲，都穆在此衹涉及此碑的研究史，不討論其書法。王澍（1668－1743）並不認可都穆把《豳州昭仁寺碑》歸爲虞世南書丹，他詳盡描述了上述二碑書風之異（《虛舟題跋》第710頁）。
金石學家錢大昕爲乾隆十九年（1754）進士，曾參與編修史志、任欽天監和鄉試考官，因仕宦的緣故，邸居山東。其生平簡介，見。他的考證學（或考據學）造詣精深，而考證學强調借助文獻學方法，以求正確解讀經書，並爲史學研究提供佐證。《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷第6頁。考據學的早期範例評述，見克維林。他參與許多大規模文獻的修纂，著述頗豐，如考史之作《二十二史考異》（1782）和多次重刻的《金石文跋尾》。

由書名可見，錢大昕的《金石文跋尾》表達了對歐陽脩（1007－1072）《集古録跋尾》（撰於1054－1072年）的敬意。歐陽脩生平簡介，見。有關歐陽脩古物研究之作及其相關文獻，見陳雲倩。艾朗諾第7－59頁指出，歐陽脩的《集古録跋尾》雖爲史學研究力作，但他更像古董鑒賞家。有關宋元文獻體系中的知識内涵，見孟絜予。同他的金石學先驅一樣，錢大昕也收藏銘刻拓本，並爲之作跋。這四個跋文集是按照工作進度的順序出版，如歐陽脩所作。本處採用的修訂版依照朝代排序，亦如歐陽脩的修訂本。不過，如跋文所示，修訂本的每個條目仍加上原屬四個跋文集的排序代號，“元”“亨”“利”“貞”。此書所收各條目旨在提供史料。錢大昕着眼史學，其中所收中正胡賓題記（徂徠山8號）條目涉及官制與職官名。

錢大昕的内弟王鳴盛（號西莊，1722－1797）乃同年進士，王氏生平見。爲1787年刻本《金石文跋尾》作序，開篇即引傅山（字青主，1607－1684或1685）傅山在早期考證學的角色見白謙慎。其簡略生平見。向閻若璩（字百詩，1636－1704）其生平見。提出的問題：金石文字足以正經史之訛而補其闕，此學始於何代何人？ 《潛研堂金石文跋尾》第18731頁。
閻若璩列舉了漢代以來的幾位學者，以此回答傅山。王鳴盛認爲，這些學者的論述“偏而不全”，往往只涉及部分甚至單一的刻銘。 《潛研堂金石文跋尾》第18731頁。 王鳴盛接着建構他的正統考證學。一般認爲，錢大昕考據超越前賢。王鳴盛也讚揚他爲“古今金石學之冠”。 《潛研堂金石文跋尾》第18731頁。 王鳴盛的序文似乎誇飾錢大昕，但仍能反映出錢大昕在學界的地位。錢大昕的博學及重要性見。
錢大昕的《泰山道里記》序提到，他在京師時已聞聶鈫之名。其同年進士李素伯（活動於18世紀）曾向他提過聶氏，稱其居岱山之陽，治學出色。乾隆三十年（1765）春，錢氏過訪泰山，邀聶鈫爲導遊，然未得謀面，許多景點因此没有找到。這年秋天，錢氏又經過泰安，李素伯爲錢氏引見聶氏，並事先致函聶氏，以確保二人會面。錢氏未遊遍泰山及鄰近諸山，但獲贈聶鈫《泰山道里記》，通讀一過，其後還徵引其資料。
錢大昕《金石文跋尾》徂徠山部分完全建立在聶鈫的基礎上。他列出兩個條目，即經名“般若波羅蜜經”（徂徠山1號）石刻及“大般若經”殘字及佛名石刻（徂徠山5、9和10號）。錢氏認爲，聶鈫是首位實地詳盡考察第二組石刻的學者。錢氏此書第二條目提及，聶鈫親製拓本並將拓本和所撰寫筆札一同寄往京師供其參考。“手拓寄予都下且詒以書。”參見下文徂徠山5號“討論”：《潛研堂金石文跋尾》第18767－18768頁。

錢大昕把聶鈫的研究納入自己的著作。這展示了學者通力合作和書信往還的重要，尤其對於錢氏宏博的研究計劃來説，其目的在於涵蓋所有已知的金石資料，特别是所有能收集到的資料。錢氏不以種類爲限，不囿於某一地域，所取宏博，這一點獲得學界讚譽。但同一時期也有其他學者有意識地以某地銘刻爲研究對象，深入探討，偶爾實地考察。例如段松苓（1745－1800）撰《山左碑目》（1796）《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷第6頁。即單列“已見”一節列敘徂徠山銘刻。同時，他參與了阮元（1764－1849）主持的《山左金石誌》（1797）。阮元强調，實地考察是史學和小學研究的基礎。《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷第3－5頁。當阮元及其幕僚移録王世貴的題記（徂徠山7號）時，首次將該址今日所知的所有題刻收録其中。

上述關於徂徠山的記述以文字爲主，間或有木版插圖，畫面簡略。但是黄易（1744－1802）關於黄易見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷雷德侯“金石學家與鄒城”一文，第5－6頁。所繪徂徠山圖景提供了新的視覺材料。其《岱巖訪古日記》簡述了1797年旅行的第十日，經過大汶口一座超過百丈（320米左右）的石橋。《岱巖訪古日記》第83頁。其《岱麓訪碑圖册》（1797）題名爲“大汶口”的一葉也描繪這一場景。 《蓬萊宿約》第193頁。此圖册二例參見《尖山圖》，以及《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷，第18頁，圖2《鐵山圖》。題跋頗具詩意，描述汶河上長而蜿蜒的石橋以及彷彿近在咫尺的徂徠山。黄易提及“曩遣工入山，搨得北齊佛經。”見下文徂徠山1號“討論”：《蓬萊宿約》第193頁。題記亦録於《虚齋名畫録》第2079頁。韓文彬第21頁也討論此段引文。這並不意味着他不願攀登艱難的山路去讀碑。

19世紀，王昶（1725－1806）其生平見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷，雷德侯“金石學家與鄒城”一文，第6頁。亦見《清代名人傳略》。《金石萃編》（1805年序）稱：“黄司馬易親至，其處言之甚詳。”見下文徂徠山1號“討論”部分：《金石萃編》第597－598頁。 王昶或許未曾親臨徂徠山，因爲他將所有的銘刻記作刻於映佛巖。簡短的描述之後，他轉録了徂徠山1、2、3、4（不過他誤列徂徠山4號的行格）、9和10號題刻的内容，並節引聶鈫《泰山道裏記》。他顧及題刻内容，在按語中做了重要闡釋。以下摘要的原文與英譯，見徂徠山1、2、3及4號“討論”部分《金石萃編》之條目。首先，他注意到徂徠山2號和3號刻字大小的差異與石塊本身無涉，而與内容有關。他不同於聶鈫及其追隨者將“經”字歸於經名，而與下面的“主”字連讀作“經主”。這樣的解釋已被當今學界認可。王昶繼而提出，這可能與左邊“王子椿”三字有關，即“王”字在上，表姓氏，“子椿”在下，爲經主名，並解釋王子椿之名前冠官銜。王昶討論普憘題記（徂徠山4號）第一行第二字，其傳録與阮元相同（見上文），但未提及阮氏。他認爲，“僧齊”應作“僧齋”。見下文徂徠山4號“討論”部分：《金石萃編》第597－598頁。目前已證實“齊”可讀作“齋”，此讀法由先秦文獻可證，但在6世紀似乎並不多見。因此，本卷不採用王昶的識讀。不過王昶在探討刻銘意義時對稀見的“觀世音佛”佛名所作評論值得注意：“是觀世音從師授記之名。”《金石萃編》第597－598頁。參見下文徂徠山“刻銘”部分，徂徠山9號，，以及徂徠山9號“討論”部分，。 傳統金石學家中，這樣解釋佛學問題的學者實屬罕見。

馮雲鵬（1765－1839）和馮雲鵷（1779－1857）兄弟的《金石索》（1821－1823）是重要的著作，這是第一部附有“北齊王子椿徂徠山摩崖”（徂徠山2和3號）及“徂徠山摩崖二”（徂徠山4號）兩張覆刻拓本的著作。作者以拓本來對證他們新的釋讀。例如，拓本中普憘題記（徂徠山4號）首行第二字明顯是“達”，他們試圖糾正聶鈫和王昶的釋讀。在二馮看來，拓本是不容辯駁的實證，但並未考慮到，拓工的無意操作有可能導致文本的不同讀法。 韓文彬第19－23頁提及古玩家往往認爲拓本準確反映了刻銘面貌，寧可在舒適的書齋中審視拓本，也不作實地踏訪。陳雲倩第44頁提及明代學者已意識到拓本僅表現某一特定階段的保存狀況，這些銘刻可能遭受風化或後人補刻。
晚清士大夫孟志青（活動於1894年左右）的題跋對拓本做出全面而精彩的鑒定。這則題跋見於一整套徂徠山拓本的浮簽，紀年爲光緒十二年四月十二日（1894年5月27日），該拓本爲孟志青舊藏，今存臺北國家圖書館。他提及見過有缺字的拓本，費時二十餘年訪求精拓。他以王昶的《金石萃編》和法偉堂（1843－1907）《山左訪碑録》對照這些拓本 《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷第8頁。，認爲法偉堂對題刻分類最清楚。
陸增祥（1833－1889）設法呈現刻銘原樣。他在其《八瓊室金石補正》（1925年刊行）中複製王子椿等題記（徂徠山6號）中“昂”字拉長的竪劃， 《八瓊室金石補正》第4342頁。另外，他在行與行之間加横綫表示换行。
其他19世紀的金石學家也著録徂徠山佛教刻銘，但往往是前人所述的沿襲或概略，且不載録其精確位置。劉聲木（1876－1959）《續補寰宇訪碑録》（1929年序）是20世紀之作，依然延續清代金石學舊説。書中有徂徠山的條目八則，首次提到佛名“大空王佛”（徂徠山10號），並且單列一條。然而，此書未附録文及詳盡的刻經址和佈局，因此，很難將題刻與書中條目一一對應。

現代徂徠山刻銘研究始於二位日本著名學者的田野調查。常盤大定（1870－1945）和関野貞（1867－1935）到中國時，正值許多日本人到此旅遊或作學術研究。在此之前，日本學者僅透過書本認識中國。有關明治晚期、大正和昭和時期日本赴中國探險家的學術追求，見傅佛果第66－190頁。兩位學者遊歷甚廣，或詳細記録，或拍攝佛教遺址，其中包括1921年徂徠山之行。發表於常盤大定第82－102頁。常盤大定和関野貞第1册：第162－163頁，圖版1－107和1－108。常盤大定第219頁。常盤大定和関野貞第11册：第72－73頁，圖版XI－75，XI－76。足立豐（1937－1986）對徂徠山刻銘的記録迄今最爲完整。 足立豐。。他轉録了全部刻銘文字及原狀佈局，還討論了幾位清代金石學家和日本學者的著作。足立豐的研究基於常盤大定和関野貞的著作，以及伏見冲敬（1917－2002）一篇附有整套拓本及照片的文章。 伏見冲敬。
上述日本學者的著述使刻銘整理臻於高峰。新近出版物涵蓋所有刻銘，但主要採用考古調查報告的形式。賴非第39－45頁。賴非第45－47頁。 本卷試圖全面討論徂徠山刻經址及其與山東其他刻經址的關係，相關材料悉數收録。

figure.head: A view from Radiant Buddha Cliff toward the valley of Guanghua Monastery; photograph taken in 2007
由映佛巖遠望光化寺山谷。2007年拍攝

div.head: 總目
div.p: 映佛巖刻有一則經名“般若波羅蜜”（徂徠山2號）、一段佛經節文（徂徠山1號）及二則題記（徂徠山3號和4號）。光化寺附近巨石上刊刻一段佛經節文（徂徠山5號）、三則佛名聯刻（徂徠山9號）、一則佛名單刻（徂徠山10號）及三則題記（徂徠山6號、7號和8號）。

div.head: 徂徠山2號
div.p: 映佛巖刻銘“般若波羅蜜”五個大字刻成豎排一行（）。其佈局類似山東其他幾處豎行佛名。陶山刻銘的“般若波羅蜜”（陶山1號）與二佛名並排刊刻，三行成一組。見本卷陶山“刻銘”，陶山1號，。陶山刻銘 “般若波羅蜜”可能當作佛名，用於持念。映佛巖刻銘“般若波羅蜜”也可能有此功能。
“般若波羅蜜”下方是經主王子椿的題名（徂徠山3號），相關的佛經節文則位於巨石下半部（徂徠山1號）。因此，“般若波羅蜜”可能指代這部佛陀回答文殊師利尋求般若波羅蜜的佛經。

div.head: 徂徠山9號
div.p: 光化寺附近巨石上三則佛名，豎行而刻，自右至左爲“彌勒佛”“阿彌陁佛”和“觀世音佛”，現僅存拓本。
彌勒佛是娑婆世界的未來佛，將於遥遠的未來降生成佛，成爲已入涅槃的釋迦牟尼佛的繼承者。彌勒佛繼承釋迦牟尼佛的主題也見於山東其他地區。洪頂山刻有一組佛名，彌勒佛（洪頂山9.9號）緊接過去七佛（洪頂山9.2－9.8號）的最後一尊佛陀，即釋迦牟尼佛之後。《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第62頁。
彌勒佛和釋迦牟尼佛於娑婆世界成等正覺，但亞洲地區净土宗尊崇的阿彌陀佛並不屬於此世界。根據流傳於印度的短篇版及長篇版《無量壽經》，阿彌陀佛居於西方無量距離的極樂土。

阿彌陀佛的名字源自梵文amita，意指“無量”，緣於此佛光明無量照耀整個宇宙；其壽命亦無量，涵蓋無量阿僧祇劫。《無量壽經》各漢譯本慮及此佛陀的這兩個特性，將他譯爲無量光佛或無量壽佛。鳩摩羅什（344－413）所譯的短篇版《無量壽經》，即《佛説阿彌陀經》，《佛説阿彌陀經》T#366，12：346b24－348b18。參見《中國佛教石經·四川省》第三卷温狄婭“《佛説阿彌陀經》”一文，第142－150頁。 影響深遠，鳩摩羅什稱此佛爲阿彌陀佛，體現其一體兩面。

山東曾多次刊刻“阿彌阤佛”佛名，此佛名或單獨出現，如大寨山（大寨山1號）和崗山（崗山D3號）的刻銘，或出現在成組的佛名中。除了徂徠山，此類成組的佛名見於洪頂山（洪頂山9.10－9.12號）、崗山（崗山D1號）和陶山（陶山1號）。大寨山上單刻佛名“阿彌陀佛”，見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第383－386頁。崗山單刻佛名“阿彌陀佛”（崗山D3號），見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷第232－233、433－435和447－450頁。出現在群組中的佛名“阿彌陀佛”，洪頂山（洪頂山9.10－9.12號），見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第193－202頁；崗山（崗山D1號），見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷第232－233和433－441頁；陶山見本卷。比較這些佛名組群，有助於更全面地理解徂徠山佛名的組合。
崗山雞嘴石北側刊刻三則佛名爲一組（崗山D1號），其中阿彌陀佛佛名排在最後。前面二佛名爲釋迦文佛和彌勒尊佛，二者皆屬於娑婆世界。而阿彌陀佛的國土位於遥遠的宇宙他方，且與釋迦牟尼佛和彌勒佛不同的是，他於此時此刻宣講佛法，因此，是正在教化眾生的佛。正因如此，雞嘴石的佛名組跨越了過去到未來所有的時間，也包含了自我們的世界至宇宙遠端的一切空間。

洪頂山北麓的佛名順序爲阿彌陀佛（洪頂山9.10號）、觀世音佛（洪頂山9.11號）、大勢至佛（洪頂山9.12號）。此三則佛名於一長排佛名（洪頂山9.1－9.15號）中形成一個子群。有關此排佛名，見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第61－62頁。眾所皆知，觀世音佛和大勢至佛是協助阿彌陀佛引導亡者至其西方净土的菩薩。這兩位菩薩於阿彌陀佛入無餘涅槃後，也將成佛並依次成爲阿彌陀佛的繼任者。因此，這三則佛名建立了阿彌陀佛系的一脈傳承。

有關這阿彌陀佛一脈的記述見《悲華經》。此經有兩個漢譯本，較早的譯本爲《大乘悲分陀利經》八卷T#158，3：233c－289a，後秦（384－417）時譯，譯者失名。另一較晚的譯本 《悲華經》十卷T#157，3：167a－233c（特别是第185－186頁），由曇無讖（385－433）譯於419年。關於此經校訂本的介紹及參考文獻，見吕朱亨第126－127頁。叙述一位國王及其1011個兒子親近寶藏如來。預言該王將於未來成佛，名“阿彌陀如來”，其長子名曰不眴，得授記名“觀世音”，並成爲阿彌陀佛的繼任者，號“遍出一切光明功德山王如來”。次子得字“大勢至”，於觀世音佛入涅槃後繼任，號“善住珍寶山王如來”。王昶《金石萃編》（1805年序）第598頁首次提及。相關研究見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第62頁。亦見于君方 ，第36頁和羅海友。《悲華經》故事的版本似乎完全符合洪頂山的佛名組群。“觀世音”和“大勢至”的如來稱號中的“山王”元素，可能是促使此佛名刻於此處摩崖的因素。
類似的故事又見於于闐沙門曇無竭譯於453年的《觀世音菩薩授記經》（T#371，12：356a－357a）。于君方第36頁。
刊刻於徂徠山這組佛名的第三位佛陀是觀世音，在此也稱爲佛。5世紀之後的實物和文獻資料證實將觀世音尊崇爲佛。龍谷大學亞洲佛教文化研究中心倉本尚德找到了兩尊紀年分别爲532年 和541年的觀世音佛造像和九則提及觀世音佛的發願文，這九則發願文的紀年最早爲495年，最晚是575年。倉本尚德。徂徠山的觀世音佛並非孤例。
總之，徂徠山三則佛名成組的主題應是佛陀傳承未來，如同彌勒菩薩將於此娑婆世界成就無上正等正覺，觀世音佛也將於西方净土繼承阿彌陀佛。

div.head: 徂徠山10號
div.p: 佛名“大空王佛”僅見於單幅拓本。根據金石文獻，原刻於徂徠山三則佛名（徂徠山9號）的左側。《金石萃編》第597－598頁。《岱覽》第4册，卷二十二，第481頁。《泰山圖誌》第215頁。由於此拓與徂徠山9號的拓片都是高105釐米，《北京圖書館藏中國歷代石刻拓本彙編》 第8册：第17頁。“大空王佛”一名可能緊鄰其他三佛名，還應看作這組佛名的一部分。
在此例中，“大空王佛”名可能列於“觀世音佛”之後。此兩則佛名的連結見於疑僞經《觀世音十大願經》。關於此經，見陶山“刻銘”，。該經叙述觀世音佛如何從名爲“空王觀世音佛”處得到他於未來成佛的授記預言。關於“大空王佛”稱號的含義，見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第60－61頁。這一複合的佛名只見於此經，確立了觀世音成佛與空的教法之間的緊密關係。

div.head: 徂徠山1號
div.p: 映佛巖刻有《文殊師利所説摩訶般若波羅蜜經》九十八字著名節文。有關此處佛經節文的討論，見本卷尖山（尖山6號），，以及水牛山（水牛山2號），。
刻經區呈横向矩形，經文14行，每行7字。因此，各行長度均等。所有刻於他處的這段節文，最後一行均未刻滿。只有刊刻於嶧山妖精洞的節文（嶧山3號）每行等長。《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷第75－83頁。妖精洞的文字安排於縱向矩形，共7行，每行14字。
山東省不同地區所刊刻的九十八字節文另一明顯區别是“無”字的寫法，或作“無”字，或如此處作簡體“无”字。

div.head: 徂徠山5號
div.p: 巨石上十七空刻銘與見於《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#223，8：217a7－424a8。的幾段經文相符。《大智度論》《大智度論》T#1509，25：57a6－756c9。大量引用這部經，所以十七空經文也見於此論。不過，刻銘起首宣稱該經文出自《大般若經》。藏經中未見與《大般若經》完全吻合的經題。

figure.head: Location of inscriptions CLS1, CLS 2, CLS 3 and CLS 4 on the boulder at Radiant Buddha Cliff
映佛巖巨石上徂徠山1號、2號、3號及4號刻銘位置示意圖

div.p: 此刻銘僅依次列出十七種不同的“空”，未加解釋。從教義來説，十七空是廣爲人知的十八空的一種異本。十八空見玄奘（約602－664年）譯《大般若波羅蜜多經》（T#220），其英譯文及解釋見鈴木大拙（貞太郎）第255－263頁。按照《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》的定義，“佛知須菩提心所念，告須菩提：六波羅蜜是菩薩摩訶薩道，三十七助道法是菩薩摩訶薩道，十八空是菩薩摩訶薩道……”（《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#223，8：407b11－14）。十七空或十八空如同六波羅蜜有關刊刻於山東省的六波羅蜜銘文，見洪頂山（洪頂山2號，《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第64和103－107頁）；平陰縣石碑（平陰1號，《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第452－458頁）；鐵山（鐵山1號，《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷第106－109和117－147頁）；尖山（尖山12號，見本卷尖山“刻銘”，及總録中尖山12號條目）。和三十七道品，有關鐵山“三十七道品”（鐵山1號）的討論，見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第二卷第90－92和117－147頁。都是大乘佛教菩薩道的一種形式。成功實踐這些修行法的結果是成就般若波羅蜜多，即自身成佛。
《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》卷一、三、五、二十二和二十四列出七個十八空的例子，這七例十八空順序略有不同。第一至第十一及第十六至第十八項是固定的，第十二至第十五項在列表中的位置却時有變動。巨石上刻經有兩處與十八空列表不同。《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》的“自相空”在刻石上改爲“自法空”，未刻“不可得空”。
不過，《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》卷二十二也遺漏了“不可得空”，只有十七空。《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#223，8：378c28－379a2。這十七空除了“性空”與“諸法空”次序倒置，與徂徠山5號刻銘完全一致。而日本石山寺藏《大智度論》寫本未倒置這兩種空的次序。《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#1509，25：655，註2和3。僅見此本收録如徂徠山5號刻銘“十七空”之排序。

然而，即使如此，石山寺藏本《大智度論》這段經文在一些“空”之前有“行” 字，該段完整的經文爲：“……行内空、行外空、行内外空、行空空、行大空、第一義空、有爲空、無爲空、畢竟空、無始空、散空、諸法空、性空、自相空、無法空、有法空、無法有法空……”（《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#223，8：378c28－379a2）。而徂徠山5號刻銘無此字。不過，巨石所刻與《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》卷二十二所收十八空細目非常接近。這十八空除了多“不可得空”之外，其次序與巨石所刻完全相符。《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#223，8：383a26－29。與此經文對應的段落見《大智度論》T#1509，25：661b3－6：“修内空、外空、内外空、空空、大空、第一義空、有爲空、無爲空、畢竟空、無始空、散空、性空、諸法空、自相空、不可得空、無法空、有法空、無法有法空壞，是修般若波羅蜜。”
十八空亦刊刻於洪頂山《摩訶衍經碑》（洪頂山5號），不過此處僅有幾字完整刻出 。原計劃刊刻的經文需經過復原。這段經文與《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》卷五所列十八空最相符。《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#223，8：250b3－7，見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第64和125－130頁。
經過比對《大正藏》各種十七或十八空，可知其彼此之間，以及與石刻十七空之間存在許多細微的差異。而這些差異尚不足以判定徂徠山刻經屬於哪個特定流派或時期。

div.head: 徂徠山3號
div.p: 徂徠山刻銘主要供養人王子椿的名字出現於映佛巖上半部，緊鄰五個大字“般若波羅蜜”（徂徠山2號）之左側。他是下方九十八字節文（徂徠山1號）的經主。王子椿的頭銜除了“梁父縣令”外，另一個是“冠軍將軍”，表明他曾參與軍事活動。

div.head: 徂徠山4號
div.p: 這則題記包括紀年“武平元年”（570）和兩位供養人的名字。題記的第一行提到一供養人名普憘，他不是僧人就是未加任何頭銜的在家居士。題記第三行稱“僧齊大衆”爲供養人。最後一行則提到主持工作的維那名叫慧遊。

div.head: 徂徠山6號
div.p: 這是光化寺附近巨石上主要的題記，緊接十七空經文之後。如同映佛巖徂徠山3號的題記，這則題記提到王子椿及其頭銜“冠軍將軍”和“梁父縣令”。此題記還列出其同爲供養人的五個兒子的名字。最後一位名“僧真”者可能是僧人。因此，徂徠山5號經文刻銘很可能由一個家族捐造。

div.head: 徂徠山7號
div.p: 王世貴之名突兀地刊刻於十七空刻經第六行的下方，也許是後纔加的。他似乎是王子椿家族的成員之一，可能不是王子椿的兒子，因爲他們的名字已刊刻於徂徠山6號題記中。

div.head: 徂徠山8號
div.p: 此題記刊刻於巨石的東面，銘文爲“中正胡賓”和紀年“武平元年”（570）。供養人的官銜爲“中正”，是指當地士大夫具有任官資格等級者。 賀凱第189頁。作爲地方官員，胡賓應與縣令王子椿熟識。
此題記或與只存拓本的佛名組（徂徠山9號和10號）有關，因部分金石資料提到此題記刻於這些佛名之後。見徂徠山8號“討論”部分，。

figure.head: Mount Culai seen from the south, as provided by Google™Earth, with inscription CLS 1–4 and CLS 5–10
徂徠山南面全景圖，徂徠山1至4號刻經群和5至10號刻經群位置示意圖。圖片來源：谷歌地球™

div.head: 刊刻技法
div.p: 徂徠山刻經採用三種技法：

div.head: Mount Culai
div.p: Remote Mount Culai 徂徠山 is far from all the other inscribed sites in Shandong. There are two clusters of engravings within this mountain range, one on top of a low peak and another in a valley, close to a monastery. The core text on the peak is the passage of 98 characters on the nature of the Buddha, often seen in Shandong. It lends itself to meditation. The donor, Wang Zichun 王子椿, was an official who acted in cooperation with the local Buddhist association. The core text in the valley lists different kinds of Emptiness as stages on the bo­dhi­sattva path. This text is seen only one other time in Shandong. In addition, four engraved Buddha names invoke dimensions of cosmic time and space and guarantee the transmission of buddhahood into the future. The donor of this cluster is also Wang Zichun. Here, however, he acts together with members of his family.

The research history on Mount Culai is involved and rewarding. The inscriptions were first mentioned in 1192, many centuries before other sutra engravings in Shandong caught the attention of epigraphers. When systematic enquiries began in the eighteenth century, a local scholar, Nie Wen 聶鈫 (1711–1796), distinguished himself by being one of the first epigraphers to search out the texts on the rock.

div.head: Location
div.p: Mount Culai is an isolated mountain range in central Shandong. To the north, it is separated from the ranges of Mount Tai 泰山山脈 by the valley of the Dawen River 大汶河, which runs in a west-southwestern direction toward Lake Dong­ping 東平湖. In the valley to the south of Mount Culai, the Chaiwen River 柴汶河 runs westward. It enters the Dawen River close to the town of Dawenkou 大汶口鎮. Mount Culai is part of the region of the city of Xintai 新泰 (map in front endpaper).

Radiant Buddha Cliff (Yingfoyan 映佛巖), an imposing upright boulder with four inscriptions (CLS 1–4; fig. ), is situated on a low peak in the southeast corner of Mount Culai’s ranges. Its geographic position is 117.3938° east and 36.0112° north WGS84. This equals 535490 E (easting) and 3985260 N (northing) on the meridian 50S UTM.

About one kilometer down the valley to the west of Radiant Buddha Cliff, is a modern monastery at the site of the ancient Guanghua Monastery (Guang­hua Si 光化寺; fig. ). Close to the path leading to the monastery, at a distance of 0.7 km from Radiant Buddha Cliff (fig. ), is a boulder, the size of a man (fig. ), at 117.3882° east and 36.0153° north WGS84. This equals 534980 E (easting) and 3985720 N (northing) on the meridian 50S UTM. On this boulder are six further inscriptions (CLS 5–10).

The two sites on Mount Culai are the most remote among the Shandong inscription sites. Closest are the Diamond Sutra in Sutra Stone Valley at Mount Tai, 34 km to the northwest, and the Buddha names on Mount Fenghuang 鳳凰山, 45 km to the southwest. These inscriptions are not visible from the inscription sites at Mount Culai, as its western and southern peaks block the view. The inscriptions on Radiant Buddha Cliff (CLS 1–4) face south-southwest, and those on the main side of the boulder close to Guanghua Monastery (CLS 5–7) face southwest. As on Mount Culai, the inscriptions on Mount Tai, Mount Fenghuang, as well as on Mount Shuiniu 水牛山, are similarly oriented toward the south.

Although Mount Culai is relatively isolated, it would have been possible to visit the inscription clusters at Dongping 東平 and Zoucheng 鄒城 by travelling along open valleys. About one hundred kilometers to the west is Lake Dongping,The inscriptions around Lake Dongping are the topic of . and further south, at a comparable distance, is the city of Zoucheng, which could have been reached by passing Mount Fenghuang, and then turning south for some forty kilometers, by way of the city of Qufu 曲阜.The inscriptions around Zoucheng are the topic of .

figure.head: The courtyard of the modern Guanghua Monastery; photograph taken in 2007
新建光化寺庭院。2007年拍攝

div.head: Topography and Spatial Grouping
div.p:
The inscriptions at the two sites on Mount Culai date from the Northern Qi 北齊 (550–577). Radiant Buddha Cliff, which some epigraphers compare to a flat screen, is an upright boulder, approximately four meters in height. It carries the passage of 98 characters (CLS 1) taken from the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī ( Sapta­śatikā­prajñā­pāramitā-sūtra , Wen­shu­shili suo shuo mohe bore boluomi jing
文殊師利所説摩訶般若波羅蜜經; T#232, 8: 731a15–21 and T#233, 8: 738a19–24).

Above the sutra passage, to the right, a part of the boulder is broken off, forming a chute just wide enough for a person to stand or sit in. Here, on a smooth, vertical part of the rock, one title “Perfection of Wisdom” (CLS 2) and two colophons (CLS 3 and CLS 4) have been engraved (fig. , fig. ).

In front of Radiant Buddha Cliff lies a large, flat boulder, inviting the visitor to take a seat facing the sutra passage (fig. , fig. ). It may have served as a meditation seat. A comparable stone seat is also seen at Mount Hongding 洪頂山, in front of HDS 16.2.See 32–36, and figs. 12–13, pp. 76–77. The Cave of the Bewitching Fairy (Yaojing Dong 妖精洞) at Mount Yi 嶧山 (Yi 3) may also have been used for meditation.See 75, fig. 25. In all three cases the passage in 98 characters is engraved.

Below in the valley, at the second site on Mount Culai, the engraved boulder is, for reasons of conservation, surrounded today by a cage-like structure (fig. ). On the rock’s smooth southern side is a passage, listing the Seventeen Kinds of Emptiness (CLS 5), followed by a donor’s colophon (CLS 6). A second donor name was added below (CLS 7; fig. ). On the boulder’s eastern side, a smaller, smooth area of rock carries another colophon (CLS 8; fig. ). Four Buddha names are said to have once been carved to its right (CLS 9 and CLS 10).

div.head: Place Name
div.p: The ranges of Mount Culai are first mentioned in “Closed Temples (Bi gong 閟宫)” in the “Hymns of Lu” (“Lu song” 魯頌) section of the Book of Odes (Shijing 詩經). 519: “The pines of Culai, the cypress of Xinfu, they were cut, were measured, into cubits, into feet. 徂來之松，新甫之柏，是斷是度，是尋是尺。” Translated in 272. In the Tang dynasty, Liu Yuxi 劉禹錫 (772–842) used the name in his poem “Stele on the Spirit Road for Lord Cui (Cui gong shendao bei 崔公神道碑)”. 84: “This kind of rafters and beams, they must originate from Culai. 如彼榱棟，必生徂徠。” They cover an area of 173 square kilometers, with 99 peaks of various sizes. In the center, the main peak, Taiping Summit (Taipingding 太平頂), rises to an height of 1027 m above sea level. 33. About 7 km to the southeast is Mount Zhai (Zhai Shan 寨山), which is mentioned in several epigraphic sources as a reference for the location of Mount Xie 薤山, a mountain east of Mount Zhai that houses Guanghua Monastery and Radiant Buddha Cliff.Nie Wen says in his 66: “... Mount Zhai. Five miles further east is Mount Xie, where the Lan Brook rises. The water flows southeast, passing west of Zhenli village. Further south it joins the Xiaowen, above which lies Guanghua Meditation Monastery 爲寨山。又東五里爲薤山，藍溪出焉。水東南流過鎮里村西，又南入小汶。其上有光化禪寺。” The distance between Mount Zhai and Mount Xie, however, differs in epigraphic sources. For example, Tang Zhongmian 唐仲冕 (1753–1827) says in 4: juan 22, 480: “Mount Xie is situated eight miles east of Mount Zhai 薤山在寨山東八里。” These sources also describe a particular cliff at Mount Xie, called Round Tile Cliff (Yuanwaya 圓瓦崖). It is said to be located to the northeast of Mount Xie. Round Tile Cliff is the original site of Great Compassion Shrine (Dabei An 大悲庵), of which only the ruins of some stone walls remain today.See the photo in 253. These ruins are located in the immediate neighborhood of the main hall of the modern Guanghua Monastery. The shape of Round Tile Cliff is clearly seen on fig. , to the left of the inscription group CLS 5–10. The epigraphers Nie Wen 聶鈫, Song Siren 宋思仁 (1730–1807), Duan Songling 段松苓 (1745–1800), Ruan Yuan 阮元 (1764–1849), Wang Chang 王昶 (1725–1806), and Fa Weitang 法偉堂 (1843–1907) all mention Great Compassion Shrine, and say that, Radiant Buddha Cliff (Yingfoyan 映佛巖) is two or three miles to the southeast.

div.head: Related Monasteries
div.p: The Guanghua Monastery, on the south-eastern slopes of Mount Culai, is said to have been built under the Northern Wei 北魏 dynasty (386–534). During the xiangfu 祥符 era (1008–1016) of the Northern Song dynasty, it was renamed Chongqing Monastery 崇慶寺.

A stele Record of the Renovation of the Guang­hua Meditation Monastery (Chongxiu Guang­hua Chan Si zhi ji 重修光化禪寺之記), was erected in 1246; it is preserved in the present Monastery. The character chan 禪 in the title emphasizes the Chan affiliation of the Monastery in the Song dynasty. The stele text says:

figure.head: The inscribed boulder close to Guanghua Monastery; photograph taken in 2007
光化寺刻石。2007年拍攝

div.quote: As for the origin of Guanghua Monastery at Culai, its foundation can be traced back as far as to the Latter Wei [Northern Wei 北魏, 386–534] dynasty. Since the Sui dynasty [581–618] it has been named Guanghua Monastery. During the three hundred years, when the Tang dynasty [618–907] governed the realm, robe and bowl were transmitted continuously [from Chan master to disciple], and this lineage never vanished. Yet, with the chaos of the Five dynasties [907–960] that followed, the monastery was abandoned. Then the Zhao family founded the Song dynasty, and, in the seventh year of the xiangfu era [1014], the monastery was restored as Chongqing [Monastery]. It stood, with the Lingyan [Monastery] 靈巖 at Mount Mingkong 明孔山, as first and second. Before long, during the turmoil of the zhenyou era [1213–1216],Refers to the Mongol invasion in 1213. the main hall and the side buildings in the compound burned to ashes, and the lineage was no more. Alas! 7: 510–511.

quote.foreign: 徂徠光化寺者，其來遠矣，始創迹於後魏，至隋朝而有光化之名。唐有天下，三百餘年，衣缽相傳，宗派不泯。繼以五代之亂，寺從而廢。趙宋開國，迨及祥符七年，復賜號‘崇慶’，而與明孔山之靈巖相爲甲乙。無何貞祐之變，殿宇堂廡盡爲灰燼，靡有孑遺。嗚呼！

div.p:
The monastery was renovated at the beginning of the Yuan dynasty (1279–1368). Today, the houses of members of the Mount Culai Forestry Center 徂徠山林場 infringe on a major part of the monastery foundations. The original extent and layout of the foundations are still unknown, as archaeological surveys have not been made. The section, “Guest at Guanghua Monastery” (Guanghua Si ke 光化寺客), in the “Collection of Strange Records” (Jiyi ji
集異記) in scroll 417 of the Extensive Records of the Taiping Era (Taiping guangji 太平廣記), first published in 978 by Li Fang 李昉 (925–996), gives a description of Guanghua Monastery; although the account is fictional, it is worth consulting. 9: 3394–3395.

figure.head: Location of inscriptions CLS 5, CLS 6, CLS 7 and CLS 8 on the inscribed boulder close to Guanghua Monastery
光化寺刻石徂徠山5號、6號、7號及8號刻銘的位置示意圖

div.p: In 1983, eight Buddhist statues were unearthed about half a kilometer to the southeast of the monastery. Among them, a stone votive stele donated by a woman, Yang Yinguang 羊銀光, was the most fully preserved (now in the Xintai Municipal Museum 新泰市博物館).See the report . The stele measures 60 cm in height. On the front are the figures of a Buddha with two accompanying bodhisattvas. The rear carries an inscription of 32 characters in four columns, reading: “[...][...]on the jiyou 己酉 day, the eight day after the renyin 壬寅 new moon of the fourth month,Traditionally the Buddha’s birthday. in the third year of the [xing]he □和 era [April, 18, 541], the Buddha’s disciple, woman of pure faith, Yang Yinguang, had a votive stele made in accordance with her heart-felt vow.” “□□□和三年四月壬寅朔八日己酉，清信女佛弟子羊銀光造像一軀，所願從心。” The first three characters in the first column are illegible, but, as stylistically the stele seems to be a work of the Eastern Wei dynasty (534–550), the three characters can be reconstructed as “Great Wei [dynasty], xing[he] [era] 大魏興.” This votive stele supports the date for the establishment of the monastery given in the “Record on the Renovation of the Guang­hua Meditation Monastery on the stele of 1246.”

div.head: Related Persons
div.p: The principal donor of the sutra passages at Mount Culai, Wang Zichun 王子椿, served as Magistrate of Liangfu County 梁父縣, to which Mount Culai belonged under the Northern dynasties. He is named in colophons CLS 3 and CLS 6. The carving at Radiant Buddha Cliff was done in collaboration with a community association that also placed a colophon on the cliff (CLS 4). By contrast, the carving on the boulder closer to Guanghua Monastery seems to have been a family donation, as Wang Zichun also credits it to five of his sons. On this boulder is the name of another local official, whom Wang Zichun certainly knew, a certain Rectifier (zhongzheng 中正) Hu Bin 胡賓 (CLS 8).See below, Historical Inscriptions.

Although Wang Zichun is not mentioned in transmitted texts, he may have been active as a Buddhist donor elsewhere. The Records of Drawing together Antiquities (Jungu lu
攈古録) by Wu Shifen 吳式芬 (1796–1856) mention an image stele in Xingsheng Monastery 興聖寺, Fei County 費縣, donated by a certain Wang Zi[...]—the last character of the name is illegible—and forty others. It was dated to April 6, 572. 34, has the following entry: “Four-sided image stele made by Wang Zi[...] and forty others in Xingsheng Monastery. Standard script. The inscription on the stele head reads ‘Donors of Xingsheng Moanstery’, all in clerical script by Ge Shi­xing. Date in one column, in seal script. Fei County, Shandong. Eighth day of the third month of the third year of the wuping era [April 6, 572]. 興聖寺王子□等四十人造四面石碑像，正書。額題‘興聖寺主’，諸葛始興隸書。紀年一行，篆書。山東費縣。武平三年三月八日。” As Fei County lies about 100 km to the southeast of Mount Culai, the donor of this image stele may well have been the same Wang Zichun.

div.head: Dating
div.p: The date in the colophon at Radiant Buddha Cliff by Wang Zichun (CLS 4) is identical to that in the colophon on the boulder near Guanghua Monastery by Hu Bin (CLS 8). Both read: “First year of the wuping era [570].” Even the calligraphy is similar.

div.head: Research History
div.p: The Buddhist inscriptions at Mount Culai were known in scholarly circles as early as the Jin 金 dynasty (1125–1234). From the eighteenth century onward, records of the inscriptions appear in print in three genres of writing, travel literature (youji 遊記),For a short account of the development of travel literature as a genre, see ; for a more concise survey, including a discussion of the fuzziness of the genre, see . local or topographic gazetteers (difangzhi 地方誌, shanzhi 山誌),According to 49, mountain gazetteers can be classified under “topographical gazetteers”. For the development of the genre, see 11–86. An impetus for compiling topographical gazetteers was local pride. A case study of the Hangzhou prefectural gazetteers is in 233. and epigraphic studies (jinshi xue 金石學, lit. “studies of [inscriptions in] metal and stone”).For an overview of the history of epigraphic studies, see 118–167 and . Generally, the latter are the major sources. Based on field work, the otherwise unknown local scholar, Nie Wen 聶鈫 (courtesy name Jianguang 劍光; 1711–1796),The name is often wrongly rendered, such as Nie Qin 聶鈙 or Nie Xu 聶敘. For the calculation of the years of his birth and death, see . wrote about the inscriptions on Mount Culai in a work that belongs to the genre of travel writing. The most important collection of epigraphic notes on Mount Culai was compiled by the famous polymath, Qian Daxin 錢大昕 (1728–1804). These two scholars collected most of the information on the locality and the engraved texts. In the twentieth century, meticulous Japanese scholarship presented the site in its entirety, for the first time making use of photography. Spanning almost a millennium, the research history of this site is a paradigm of epigraphic studies in Shandong.

figure.head: Model of the boulder close to Guanghua Monastery with CLS 8 adjoining the main face of the boulder with inscriptions CLS 5–7
光化寺刻石模型，正面爲徂徠山5號至7號刻銘，側面爲徂徠山8號刻銘

div.p: The first mention of Buddhist texts at Mount Culai is in an inscription by An Shengqing 安升卿 (fl. twelfth century) dated to the first summer month of 1192. It shows that the site was already known in a period that witnessed a growing interest in local history.The formative period of local history writing in the Song and Yuan dynasties, taking Wuzhou 婺洲 as a case study, is discussed in . An Shengqing’s inscription was carved in regular script on a rock called Folding Screen Cliff (Pingfeng­yan 屏風岩); it is no longer extant. 240 illustrates a screen-shaped boulder, bearing the inscription “Folding Screen Rock (Pingfengshi 屏風石)”, located in the present Scenic Area of Sixteen Valleys (Shiliuyu jingqu 十六峪景區), a few miles west of Guanghua Monastery. The characters are said to be approximately 70 cm in diameter. It is not clear, however, whether this is the very rock, on which An Shengqing wrote. That one, called Folding Screen Cliff (Pingfengyan), appears on Tang Zhongmian’s (1753–1827) map of Mount Culai, 4: juan 22, 475 in the background, to the left of Guanghua Monastery. From the Song dynasty onward, scholars with historical or antiquarian interests sought inscriptions in the countryside, and often obtained rubbings as trophies. This activity was known as “seeking out ancient stelae (fang gubei 訪古碑)”, a practice which later became popular in the Ming and Qing dynasties.For the early phase, see 173; for the Ming and Qing periods, see 49. Occasionally the visitors left inscriptions commemorating their trips, as An Shengqing had done. 154–179 discusses the practice of literati visiting and inscribing places in the Song dynasty. He had briefly noted that he and his fellows “roamed over Mount Culai to explore the ancient remains”, and “obtained [a rubbing] of an army general’s stone sutra in the Buddha Valley (Fogu 佛谷)”. 4: juan 22, 490: “安升卿題名：……游徂徠探古迹，……得冠軍石經於佛谷，……明昌壬子孟夏記。” The “army general (guanjun 冠軍)” was almost certainly Wang Zichun 王子椿, whose name appears in two colophons (CLS 3 and CLS 5). Because the name “Buddha Valley” is not mentioned in other sources, it is not exactly clear, which inscriptions An Shengqing saw.

In the eighteenth century, Qing dynasty scholars made detailed notes and transcriptions of the inscriptions at Mount Culai. This period saw the publication of works in all three genres mentioned above, travel literature, gazetteers, and epigraphic studies. While the former mention the inscriptions within the running text and identify the location of the sites, giving distances and topographical information, the latter provide structured references in the form of a catalog. Gazetteers fall in between these two, featuring topographical information, but also separate entries in catalog style. Although in each instance the purpose and framing is different, type and arrangement of the information provided for the inscriptions are similar: Content, dating, calligraphic style, their topographical position, and their location within administrative units.

The three genres offer a framework within which to explore the general intellectual, socio-political, and technological preconditions for writing the texts. Yet only the actions and interactions of the individual writers give us information on the historical context, in which the inscriptions were produced.

For example, local gazetteers were composed as early as the Song dynasty, taking advantage of the development of printing. They were sources for numerous and voluminous later gazetteers, as the production costs dropped significantly in the sixteenth century.For the issue of financing local gazetteers in the early phase, see 124–128; for late imperial China, see 213–248. It was during this time that the first mountain gazetteers were published for the greater region of Mount Tai, of which Mount Culai was a part. But neither Wang Ziqing’s 汪子卿 Gazetteer of Mount Tai (Tai Shan zhi 泰山誌) of 1555, nor Zha Zhilong’s 查志隆 History of Dai (Dai shi 岱史) of 1587, . both privately compiled, mention the Buddhist inscriptions at Mount Culai. Only when, in the Qianlong 乾隆 era (1736–1796), official surveys were initiated, involving knowledgeable local scholars, did information about the Buddhist inscriptions become available in print.

The most eminent individual in this process was Nie Wen. Little is known about him, except that he grew up at the foot of Mount Tai and became a renowned expert of local culture. As we shall see in more detail below, his work illustrates the importance of local knowledge for large-scale projects, such as the compilation of epigraphic entries for gazetteers. When, in 1748, Nie Wen guided the official Liu Lüfu 劉履夫 (active 1743–1748) in exploring imperial roads and ancient remains, the latter composed Brief Notes on Mount Tai (Tai Shan jilüe 泰山紀略) and gave the manuscript to him.See 23.

In 1760, the official Cheng Cheng 成城 (1713–?) harvested the information from the Brief Notes on Mount Tai for the Tai’an Prefectural Gazetteer (Tai’an fuzhi 泰安府誌).For the editorial process in compiling gazetteers, see 145–152. In the epigraphic section (jinshi zhi 金石誌) of this gazetteer, three entries record inscriptions on Mount Culai, a division which seems to have been chosen on account of the location of the inscriptions, each group being on a separate rock: First, the “Northern Qi Great Wisdom Sutra stone inscription” (Bei Qi Da bore jing shike 北齊大般若經石刻; CLS 5, 6, 8), on a giant boulder located near Guang­hua Monastery; second, the “Northern Qi Buddha Names Stone Carvings” (Bei Qi foming shike 北齊佛名石刻; CLS 9, 10), on a rock next to the first group; and third, the “Northern Qi stone inscription of the Perfection of Wisdom Sutra” (Bei Qi Bore boluomi jing shike 北齊般若波羅蜜經石刻; CLS 2, 3, 1, 4, in Cheng Cheng’s sequence), located at Radiant Buddha Cliff. Cheng Cheng did not transcribe the Buddha names, nor the sutra passage, CLS 1, which he says is severely weathered. 229.

The first mountain gazetteer (shanzhi 山誌) that mentions the inscriptions at Mount Culai was Zhu Xiaochun’s 朱孝純 (juren 1762) Illustrated Gazetteer of Mount Tai (Tai Shan tuzhi 泰山圖誌; preface 1774). When Zhu Xiaochun was sent to Tai’an as Prefect (zhifu 知府), he considered it his duty to supplement earlier accounts on Mount Tai. He was very proud of his locality and wanted to record all noteworthy aspects of nature or culture that he came across, to prevent them from falling into oblivion or disappearing by force of nature or through bookworms. 3. In his detailed topographical description of the mountain range and its landmarks, he only notes the sutra titles. He also added an appendix (fu 附) to his section on epigraphy (jinshi 金石), entitled “Epigraphica at the Culai Mountains and Fragmentary Inscriptions” (“Culai zhu­shan jinshi ji suike” 徂徠諸山金石及碎刻). Here he lists three entries on the three inscription groups, as in the local gazetteers mentioned above. He seems to have been aware of addenda and corrections that Nie Wen was to publish later and included these.

By far the most voluminous mountain gazetteer of Mount Tai is Tang Zhongmian’s 唐仲冕 (1753–1827) Survey of Dai (Dai lan 岱覽). He devotes the entire fascicle 22 to Mount Culai. Tang Zhongmian was a palace examinee, holding the degree of Presented Scholar (jinshi 進士), but he never obtained a government position. While he was director at the Mount Tai Academy (Tai Shan shuyuan 泰山書院), in 1782, he began the work on his Survey, completing it in 1793. Unlike Zhu Xiaochun, he did not place topographical descriptions and inscriptions into different sections. His three entries on the Mount Culai engravings are preceded by brief descriptions of their relative distances from other landmarks. Tang Zhongmian not only mentioned the four Buddha names (CLS 9, 10) as Cheng Cheng had done, but also transcribed them. He is also the first to identify the calligraphy as being in symmetrical clerical script (bafen 八分書). Furthermore, he notes that the first year of the wuping 武平 era corresponds to the sixth year of tiantong 天統 era, which was a gengyin 庚寅 year (570).Both are era names of emperor Gao Wei 高緯 (r. 557–577). According to the official histories, the tiantong era lasted only five years.

Nie Wen, who was dissatisfied with the local gazetteers, especially with some parts of Cheng Cheng’s Tai’an Prefectural Gazetteer, listed errors and omissions in the preface to his An Itinerary of Mount Tai (Tai Shan daoli ji 泰山道里記, 1772). These errors spurred him on to go out into the field, a task he undertook with great enthusiasm, as he vividly describes in his preface:
div.quote:
And when I arrived there, I erected ladders to look closely, and cleaned the stone surface in order to investigate [the inscriptions] thoroughly; in wind, rain, cold and heat, not sparing any effort, I enquired of local farmers, checked a stack of books, exhausting my best years. 近乃豎梯，親加刮摩以核之。雖風雨寒暑，弗憚其勞。而又質之野老，參考群書，竭半生精力。
23.
div.p: From 1765 to 1772, Nie Wen put together four drafts of the Itinerary, before he gave it its final form, and yet, he continued to regard it as unfinished. Its readers praised it as skilfully written, “using the routes as guiding principles” “以絡爲綱。”Postface by Nie Xuewen 聶學文 (juren 1760), dated 1766, 75. of the narrative. By “making the texture of the mountain the texture of the text”, “以山之脈絡爲文之脈絡。” Preface by Xu Zonggan 徐宗幹 (1796－1866), dated 1826, 24. Nie Wen created the experience of climbing the mountain for those reading the text:
div.quote: Therefore, if someone indulges in recumbent travel with this very book, then he is not unlike Jianguang [Nie Wen], who with his shoes of silver grass and his bamboo staff, climbed five thousand leagues (ren 仞).Preface by Gao Yi 高怡 (fl. 18th century), dated 1773, 28: “或即藉是以當臥游，則又不異與劍光芒鞋竹杖，登陟五千仞間也。” For other references to “recumbent travel (woyou 臥游)” in monastery gazetteers, see 54; for a short historical introduction on the genre, see 3–7.
div.p: The impact of the Itinerary on later scholars is evident in its array of eight prefaces, one of them written by Qian Daxin (see below). This philological-historical journey through a cultural landscape became a favoured piece of travel literature, a guide for visitors, as well as a standard reference for scholars with antiquarian interests.

By closely examining the inscriptions himself, Nie Wen went beyond the gazetteers in three ways: First, he added a transcription of the sutra passage, CLS 1; second, he explicitly corrected the misreading of the first two characters of the Colophon by Hu Bin (CLS 8) in Cheng Zhilong’s 程志隆 Tai’an County Gazetteer (Tai’an Xian zhi 泰安縣誌; 1760), which came out in the same year as the Tai’an Prefectural Gazetteer; and third, he supplied the first two characters, puxi 普憘 in the Colophon by Puxi and others (CLS 4) and recognized four, instead of three, characters in the fourth column. The reading of these particular characters was a constant issue, in which some followed Nie Wen, probably because he was trusted, being an early and reliable eye witness, whereas others refrained from a final judgement due to insufficient evidence.

Nie Wen’s major analytical advance was his recognition of the similarity of the calligraphic style of the Sutra on the Perfection of Wisdom on Radiant Buddha Cliff (CLS 1) and of the monumental Diamond Sutra in Stone Sutra Valley at Mount Tai (Tai 1), a point also emphasized by Nie Wen’s nephew, Nie Xuewen 聶學文 (juren 1760), in the 1766 postface (ba
跋) to the Itinerary. 75.

Nie Wen cites Wang Shimao 王世懋 (1536–1588),For the dates and a short biography, see . who, a century earlier, had argued against the popular attribution of the Diamond Sutra to the celebrated calligrapher Wang Xizhi 王羲之 (303–361) and had dated it to the Song or Yuan dynasties. But Nie Wen questioned Wang Shimao’s conjecture by referring to Mount Culai.See CLS 1, Discussion (18767), p. . The sutra passage on Mount Culai (CLS 1) had a secure date, because its donor, Wang Zichun, was known to have been active in the sixth century. In a remarkable early exercise of stylistic analysis, Nie Wen argued that the clerical script at Mount Culai was similar to that of the Diamond Sutra engraving and concluded that they were both from the same hand, namely that of Wang Zichun. Later, Qian Daxin, enthusiastically endorsed this stylistic argument on the unquestioned assumption that the donor also was the calligrapher. 38 investigates another example of an interest in calligraphic style. As a first step she cites Du Mu’s 都穆 (1459–1525) Gems of the Golden Scallion (Jinxie linlang 金薤琳琅, undated). In his discussion of the Zhaoren Monastery Stele in Binzhou (Binzhou Zhaoren si bei 豳州昭仁寺碑) Du Mu followed Zheng Qiao 鄭樵 (1104–1162) in attributing the calligraphy to the famous Yu Shinan 虞世南 (558–638), but he puts aside the differences in style between the Zhaoren Monastery Stele and that of the Confucius Temple Stele (Kongzi miaotang bei 孔子廟堂碑), which is accepted as being by Yu Shinan. Sena argues that, in this case, Du Mu merely presented a history of the study on the stele, but did not intend to discuss its calligraphy. In order to undermine the claim that Yu Shinan was the calligrapher of the Zhaoren Monastery Stele, as Du Mu implied, Wang Shu 王澍 (1668–1743) subsequently presented a full description of stylistic differences in the execution of the characters on the two stelae (710).

The epigrapher Qian Daxin had obtained the highest degree of an Presented Scholar in 1754 and subsequently received several appointments, as a contributor to history projects, as an assistant to the Imperial Board of Astronomy (Qintianjian 欽天監), and as examiner for the civil service examination, for which duty he resided in Shandong.For a biographical sketch, see . He was one of the leading scholars in the field of evidential learning (kao­zheng xue 考證學 or kaoju xue 考據學), which emphasized the importance of philological methods for the correct interpretation of the Classics, thereby gaining evidence for historical studies.See 21. For a useful review of the early standard literature on evidential learning, see . He pursued many scholarly projects, resulting in multi-volume works, such as the philological-historical Inquiry on Discrepancies in the Twenty-two Dynastic Histories (Nian’er shi kao yi 廿二史考異, 1782) and the Postscripts to Epigraphic Texts (Jinshi wen bawei 金石文跋尾), which went through several editions.

The title of Qian Daxin’s Postscripts to Epigraphic Texts was an homage to Ouyang Xiu’s 歐陽脩 (1007–1072) Postscripts to the Records of Collecting Antiquities (Jigu lu bawei 集古録跋尾; written 1054–1072).For a short biography of Ouyang Xiu, see . For his antiquarian writings and related literature, see . 7–59, argues, that Ouyang Xiu’s Postscripts were important for historical studies, but that he rather presents himself as a connoisseur of antiquities. For the epistemic implications embodied in the organization of different Song and Yuan works, see . Like his predecessors, Qian Daxin collected rubbings of inscriptions and, over time, wrote postscripts on those in his collection.He first published four collections of postscripts in the order in which he worked on them, similarly to Ouyang Xiu. Yet the revised edition, used here, was rearranged according to dynasties, as Ouyang Xiu’s work later was. But as described in the postface, the original arrangement in four collections was retained by inserting for each entry one of four enumerative characters, yuan 元, heng 亨, li 利, and zhen 貞. The fundamental purpose of the entries was to provide historical evidence. Qian Daxin demonstrates his historiographical interests in the section on the colophon by the Rectifier Hu Bin (CLS 8), where he comments on the systems of administrative terminology and official titles.

In a preface to the Postscripts edition of 1787, Qian’s brother in law and a fellow successful palace examinee of 1754, Wang Mingsheng 王鳴盛 (courtesy name Xizhuang 西莊; 1722–1797),For a biography, see . begins with a question by Fu Shan 傅山 (courtesy name Qingzhu 青主; 1607–1684 or 1685)Fu Shan’s role in the early phase of evidential learning is discussed in ; for a short biography see . to Yan Ruoqu 閻若璩 (courtesy name Baishi 百詩; 1636–1704).For a biography, see . Quoting this exchange, he harks back to the early paragons of evidential learning and the scholarly purpose of their work:
div.quote:
Inscriptions on metal and stone allow us to rectify errors in the Classics and Histories and to fill in their omissions. In which dynasty and with whom did this [kind of] learning begin? 金石文字足以正經史之訛而補其闕，此學始於何代何人？
18731.
div.p: In his answer, Yan Ruoqu lists some scholars from the Han dynasty onward, about whose work Wang Mingsheng then concludes that, these are all “biased and not comprehensive偏而不全”,Ibid. for they only treat certain groups of inscriptions, or even just single works. Wang Mingsheng then goes on to construct what he deems to be a proper tradition of evidential learning.

Qian Daxin, who is said to have surpassed all who had gone before him in comprehensiveness, is lauded by Wang Mingsheng as “the crowned [king] of epigraphic studies in past and present 古今金石學之冠”.Ibid. Notwithstanding the eulogistic character of Wang Mingsheng’s preface, it reflects quite well the standing of Qian Daxin within the scholarly community.The broad scope of Qian Daxin’s scholarship and its significance is discussed in .

In his preface to the Itinerary, Qian Daxin says that he had already heard about Nie Wen during his stay in the capital. Li Subo 李素伯 (fl. 18th century), a fellow palace examinee, had told Qian that Nie Wen was a fine scholar, living at the southern side of Mount Tai. When Qian visited Mount Tai in the spring of 1765, he deliberately sought out Nie Wen to act as his guide. Yet he did not meet him and, therefore, there were many sites he could not find. In the autumn, when Qian passed through Tai’an once more, Li Subo introduced him to Nie Wen, sending a letter in advance, so that Qian would be sure to get to know him. Although Qian Daxin did not himself walk over the ranges of Mount Tai and its adjacent mountains, he obtained a copy of the Itinerary, studied it thoroughly, and subsequently made use of its information.

When, in his Postscripts to Epigraphic Texts, Qian Daxin turns his attention to Mount Culai, he depends completely on Nie Wen. He gives two epigraphic entries, entitled “Sutra on the Perfection of Wisdom (Bore boluomi jing 般若波羅蜜經)” (CLS 1) and “Fragmentary characters of the Sutra on the Great [Perfection of] Wisdom and Buddha Names (Da bore boluomi jing canzi ji foming 大般若經殘字及佛名)” (CLS 5, 9, 10). According to Qian Daxin, Nie Wen was the first to investigate closely the inscriptions of the second group. In his second entry Qian mentions that Nie Wen made rubbings himself and sent them to the capital with accompanying notes supplying the necessary background. “手拓寄予都下且詒以書。” See CLS 5, Discussion (18767–18768).

By including Nie's findings in his work, Qian shows just how important scholarly collaboration and epistolary networks were for the advancement of such an ambitious comprehensive project as Qian’s, in which he aimed at covering all known epigraphic material, especially all that he, himself, could gather together. While Qian was lauded for his comprehensiveness—he covered all categories of inscriptions and did not confine himself to one geographical region—, other epigraphers consciously narrowed their geographical focus, treating only a single region exhaustively, which occasionally also involved studies in situ. This is exemplified by Duan Songling’s 段松苓 (1745–1800) List of Stelae in Shandong (Shanzuo beimu 山左碑目, 1796)See 21. , where he enumerates the inscriptions on Mount Culai under the rubric “seen by myself (ji jian 己見)”. Duan Songling was part of a team compiling the Epigraphic Records of Shandong (Shanzuo jinshi zhi 山左金石誌, 1797) led by Ruan Yuan 阮元 (1764–1849), who also emphasized the importance of solid fieldwork as the basis for historical and philological studies.See 14–17. By the time that Ruan Yuan and his collaborators had transcribed the colophon by Wang Shigui 王世貴 (CLS 7), for the first time, all the inscriptions at the site, which are known today, had entered the literature.

While the accounts mentioned above were primarily textual in nature, sometimes accompanied by simple woodblock illustrations, Huang Yi 黄易 (1744–1802),For Huang Yi, see , Lothar Ledderose, “Epigraphers of Zoucheng”, 18–21. with his paintings, added a visual dimension to the cultural landscapes of Mount Culai. In his travel account, Diary of Enquiries into Ancient Sites on Cliffs Around Mount Dai (Daiyan fanggu riji 岱巖訪古日記), he briefly notes that, on the tenth day of his journey in 1797 he crossed a stone bridge at Dawenkou 大汶口, more than a hundred zhang 丈 (appr. 320 meters) in length.See , 83. In his album, Illustrations of Enquiries into Stelae Around Mount Dai (Dailu fangbei tu 岱麓訪碑圖, 1797), a leaf entitled “Dawenkou” invokes the atmosphere of the scene. 193. For other leafs of this album see also “Sketch of Mount Jian”, fig. and “Sketch of Mount Tie” , fig. 2, p. 18. In his accompanying colophon, Huang Yi poetically describes the long, meandering bridge over the Wen River with Mount Culai in the background, appearing very close. He says that he “sent workers into the mountains to make rubbings of Buddhist sutras of the Northern Qi”.See CLS 1, Discussion (193): “曩遣工入山，搨得北齊佛經。” The colophon is also transcribed in 2079. This passage is also discussed in 21. Huang, however, did not shun the arduous mountain paths to see the inscriptions himself.

In the nineteenth century, Wang Chang 王昶 (1725–1806)On Wang Chang, see , Lothar Ledderose, “Epigraphers of Zoucheng”, 21–22; . in his Assembled Edition of Epigraphica (Jinshi cuibian 金石萃編; preface 1805) mentions that “The Commander Huang Yi went there himself, and gave a very detailed description of the site”.See CLS 1, Discussion , p. (597–598). Wang Chang probably did not go to Mount Culai, as he places all the inscriptions in a single group at Radiant Buddha Cliff. After a short description, he transcribes CLS 1, 2, 3, 4, (though his arrangement of the columns of CLS 4 is incorrect), 9, and 10, and quotes Nie Wen, albeit incompletely. However, in his annotations (an 按), he makes important observations, because he considers the meaning of the texts.For a literal translation of the text paraphrased below, see the respective entries in CLS 1, 2, 3, 4, Discussion. First he notes that, the differences in the sizes of the characters of CLS 2 and 3 are not a consequence of the constraints of the stone, but can be explained by their content. Here, unlike Nie Wen and his followers, Wang posits that, the character “sutra” (jing 經) does not belong to the title of a sutra, but together with the character zhu 主 below, reads “Sutra Donor” (jing­zhu 經主). This interpretation is accepted today. Wang Chang further states that these two characters are probably related to the three characters to the left. “Wang 王” above “Zichun 子椿”, are the family and given names of the donor, respectively. He then explains that the name is preceded by official titles. In CLS 4, the Colophon by Puxi, Wang Chang discusses the heavily weathered second character of the first column; he gives the same transcription of its legible right part as Ruan Yuan, but does not refer to him. He further argues that “‘the sangha together with (seng qi 僧齊)’ should be corrected to ‘the sangha offering a vegetarian meal (seng zhai
僧齋)’.”See CLS 4, Discussion (597–598): “‘僧齊’當作‘僧齋’字。” Although the character now pronounced “qi 齊” can be read as “zhai” and this reading is attested in earlier, mainly pre-Han sources, it seems rare as an alternative character in sixth century inscriptions. We therefore do not accept Wang Chang’s reading. Yet Wang Chang, in his quest to understand the meaning of the text, makes a remarkable comment on the unusual name Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds It is the name that [the bodhisattva] Perceiver of the World’s Sounds received from the Master as a prophecy [for his future Buddhahood]. 是觀世音從師授記之名。 597–598. See also below, Inscriptions, CLS 9, pp. , and Discussion, CLS 9, p. . Such an explanation of a buddhological issue is indeed rare among traditional epigraphers.

Feng Yunpeng 馮雲鵬 (1765–1839) and Feng Yunyuan’s 馮雲鵷 (1779–1857) Examination of Epigraphica (Jinshi suo 金石索; 1821–1823) is significant, because it is the first work to include woodblock reproductions of two rubbings of Mount Culai inscriptions, entitled “Polished cliff inscription at Mount Culai by Wang Zichun of the Northern Qi dynasty (Bei Qi Wang Zichun Culai Shan moya 北齊王子椿徂来山摩崖)” (CLS 2, 3) and “The second inscription at Mount Culai (Culai Shan moya er 徂来山摩崖二)” (CLS 4). The authors use the reproduced rubbings to justify their new readings. For example, for the disputed second character in the Colophon by Puxi (CLS 4) their reproduction clearly shows “da 達”; with this reading they attempted to correct Nie Wen and Wang Chang. They seem to take this as conclusive evidence, not appreciating that the artisans who worked on the rubbing may have unintentionally created a different reading of the character on the stone. 19–23 discusses the issues, and says that antiquarians often regarded rubbings as exact copies of the inscription on the rock and preferred to examine them in the comfort of their studios instead of going into the field. 44 shows that Ming scholars were already aware that a rubbing could present a specific stage of preservation, and that inscriptions could be weathered or recut.

A colophon by the late Qing scholar official, Meng Zhiqing 孟志青 (fl. 1894), dated to the twelfth day of the fourth month of the twentieth year of the Guangxu 光緒 era (May 27th, 1894), is a fine example of systematic connoisseurship on rubbings. It is written on a separate sheet of paper and kept with a complete set of rubbings of Mount Culai, which he had owned, now preserved in the National Central Library, Taibei. Meng Zhiqing states that he saw other rubbings with many omissions, and that it took him more than 20 years to find good quality specimens. When he checked these against Wang Chang’s Assembled Edition of Epigraphica and Fa Weitang’s 法偉堂 (1843–1907) Record of Enquiries into Stelae in Shandong (Shanzuo fangbei lu 山左訪碑録),See 25. he found that the latter provided the best organization of the inscriptions.

Lu Zengxiang 陸增祥 (1833–1889) is innovative in his efforts to convey the original design. In his Supplementary Corrigenda to the Epigraphica of Eight Gems Studio (Baqiong shi jinshi buzheng 八瓊室金石補正; published 1925) he reproduces in print the elongated vertical strokes of the character ang 昂 in the Colophon by Wang Zichun and Others (CLS 6).See 4342. He also inserts horizontal strokes between characters to indicate line breaks.

Other nineteenth century epigraphers list the Buddhist inscriptions on Mount Culai, but generally only repeat and abbreviate former accounts, and often omit the information on the exact locations. Although written in the twentieth century, Liu Shengmu’s 劉聲木 (1876–1959) Sequel to the Record of Enquiries into Stelae in the Whole World (Xubu Huanyu fangbei lu 續補寰宇訪碑録; preface 1929) still belongs to the Qing epigraphic tradition. It gives a total of eight entries on Mount Culai and is the first to list the name, Buddha King of Great Emptiness (Da kong wang fo 大空王佛; CLS 10), separately. However, as the author neither gives transcriptions, nor provides sufficient information on location and arrangement, it is difficult to correlate the surviving inscriptions with his entries.

figure.head: A flat rock, suited for meditation, in front of the boulder with the engraved sutra passage; photograph taken in 2007
映佛巖佛經節文前適於禪坐的平坦岩石。2007年拍攝

div.p: Modern research began with the fieldwork of two eminent Japanese scholars, Tokiwa Daijō 常盤大定 (1870–1945) and Sekino Tadashi 関野貞 (1867–1935). They visited China in a period when many Japanese for scholarly and general purposes explored the land that previously they had only known in books.For the scholarly pursuits of Japanese travellers to China in the late Meiji, Taishō and early Shōwa eras, see 66–190. During their extensive expeditions through several provinces, Tokiwa and Sekino made detailed notes and took photographs of Buddhist sites, including Mount Culai, which they climbed in 1921.Published in 82–102; vol. 1, 162–163, plates I–107 and I–108; 219; vol. 11, 72–73, plates XI-75 and XI-76. The most complete account on Mount Culai’s inscriptions to date is an essay by Adachi Yutaka 足立豐 (1937–1986). . He transcribes all inscriptions in their layout on the rock. He also discusses the works of a selected number of Qing epigraphers, and Japanese scholars. Adachi built upon the work of Tokiwa and Sekino, and an essay by Fushimi Chūkei 伏見冲敬 (1917–2002), which has a full set of rubbings, and photographs. .

With these Japanese contributions, the documentation of the inscriptions reached a high point. Recent publications list single inscriptions, but they remain in the form of archaeological reports. 39–45. 45–47.
Here we aim at a comprehensive discussion of the entire site and its relation with other sites in Shandong, and we include comparative material.

div.head: Inventory
div.p: Radiant Buddha Cliff carries one title “Perfection of Wisdom” (CLS 2), one sutra passage (CLS 1), and two colophons (CLS 3 and CLS 4). On the boulder near Guanghua Monastery are one sutra passage (CLS 5), a group of three Buddha names (CLS 9), a single Buddha name (CLS 10), and three colophons (CLS 6, CLS 7, CLS 8).

div.head: The Perfection of Wisdom and Buddha Names
CLS 2
div.p: On Radiant Buddha Cliff, five large characters in one column read “Perfection of Wisdom” ( prajñā­pāramitā ; bore boluomi 般若波羅蜜; fig. ). The layout resembles that of vertically written Buddha names at several other Shandong sites.

At Mount Tao 陶山, the Perfection of Wisdom (TS 1.3) is carved alongside two Buddha names, making a group of three.See Mount Tao, Inscriptions, TS 1 in the present volume, pp. . There, the Perfection of Wisdom is invoked as a Buddha name. This may also be the case for the Radiant Buddha Cliff inscription.

Below the carving is the signature of the Sutra Donor (jingzhu 經主) Wang Zichun (CLS 3), referring to the sutra passage on the lower half of the boulder (CLS 1). Thus, Perfection of Wisdom may also, pars pro toto, represent this sutra text, in which the Buddha responds to Mañjuśrī’s quest for the Perfection of Wisdom.

div.head: CLS 9
div.p: On the boulder near Guanghua Monastery, three Buddha names, now only preserved in rubbings, were engraved, from right to left, in three vertical columns. Buddha Maitreya (Mile fo 弥勒佛), Buddha Amitābha (Amituo fo 阿弥陀陁佛) and Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (Guanshiyin fo 觀世音佛). Buddha Maitreya is the future Buddha of our world-system and will succeed, albeit in a very distant future, Buddha Śākyamuni, who has already entered parinirvāṇa . Maitreya’s succession to Śākya­muni is also presented elsewhere in Shandong, in the row of Buddha names on Mount Hong­ding. There, Maitreya (HDS 9.9) follows after Buddha Śākyamuni, who is the last in the group known as the Seven Buddhas of the Past (HDS 9.2–9.8).See 85.

Maitreya and Śākyamuni will achieve awakening while dwelling in our world, but Buddha Amitābha 阿彌陀佛, who is venerated across Asia in all Pure Land schools 净土宗, does not belong to our own world-system. According to the Sukhāvatīvyūha-sūtras , transmitted in India in a shorter and a longer version, this Buddha presides over the land Sukhāvatī—in Chinese often rendered as Land of Ultimate Bliss (Jile tu 極樂土)—a pure land located at an unfathomable distance in the west.

Amitābha’s name derives from the Sanskrit amita, meaning “measureless,” because his immeasurable light shines through the entire cosmos, and because his immeasurable life-span encompasses incalculable cosmic ages. Chinese translations of the Sukhāvatī­vyūha-sūtras take these two aspects of the Buddha into account when they translate Amitābha as Buddha of Immeasurable Light (Wuliangguang fo 無量光佛), and Amitāyus as Buddha of Immeasurable Life (Wuliangshou fo 無量壽佛). The influential translation of the shorter Sukhāvatīvyūha-sūtra by Kumārajīva (鳩摩羅什; 344–413), the Sutra Spoken by the Buddha on Amitābha
(Fo shuo Amituo jing 佛説阿彌陀經), however, fused both names by taking them to be two aspects of the same Buddha, Amituo 阿彌陀.T#366, 12: 346b24–348b18. See also the article “The Amitābha Sutra” by Claudia Wenzel in 151–165.

At the Shandong sites, the name Amitābha Buddha 阿弥陀佛 was carved several times, either by itself, as at Mount Dazhai 大寨山 (DZ 1) and Mount Gang (GS D3), or in a group. In addition to Mount Culai, such groups are found at Mount Hongding (HDS 9.10–HDS 9.12), Mount Gang 崗山 (GS D1), and Mount Tao (TS 1).For the single name Amitābha Buddha see 383–386 for Mount Dazhai, and 250, 433–435 and 447–450 for Mount Gang (GS D3). For the name as part of a group see 193–202 for Mount Hongding (HDS 9.10–HDS 9.12), 250 and 433–441 for Mount Gang (GS D1), and the present volume for Mount Tao. A comparative look at these groups will lead to a fuller understanding of the combination of names at Mount Culai.

At Mount Gang, Buddha Amitābha is the last in a group of three Buddhas (GS D1) carved on the northern side of Chicken Beak Rock (Jizuishi 雞嘴石). He is preceded by Buddha Śākyamuni (Shijiawen fo 釋迦文佛) and Buddha Honored Maitreya (Mile zun fo 弥勒尊佛), who both, as mentioned, belong to our world-system. Amitābha, whose remote realm is in another corner of the universe, is also, unlike Śākyamuni and Maitreya, preaching the Dharma at this very moment. He is thus active now as a Buddha. In this way, the Buddha names at Chicken Beak Rock span all time, from the past to the future, and all space, from our own world to the far end of the cosmos.

In the sequence of Buddha names on the northern slope at Mount Hongding, Buddha Amitābha (HDS 9.10) is followed by the Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (Avalokiteśvara; Guanshiyin fo 觀世音佛, HDS 9.11) and the Buddha Great Power Obtained (Mahāsthāmaprāpta; Dashizhi fo 大勢至佛, HDS 9.12). These three names form a sub-group in the long row of Buddha names (HDS 9.1–9.15) on that mountain.On this row of Buddha names, see 84–86. Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds and Buddha Great Power Obtained are known to a wide audience as bodhisattvas (pusa
菩薩), who assist Buddha Amitābha in welcoming the deceased to his Pure Land. The two bodhisattvas will, as fully awakened Buddhas (fo 佛), one after the other, succeed Amitābha, once he has entered parinirvāṇa . Their names thus establish a line of succession to Amitābha’s buddhahood.

The narration on this succession is found in the canonical Sutra of the Compassionate Lotus Flower ( Mahākaruṇāpundarīka-sūtra ). It was translated twice into Chinese,The older translation in eight scrolls by an anonymous translator of the Latter Qin dynasty (後秦 384–417) is known as the Mahayana Sutra of the Compassionate Lotus Flower (Dasheng beifentuoli jing
大乘悲分陀利經; T#158, 3: 233c–289a); a later translation in ten scrolls compiled in 419 by Dharmaksema (Tanwuchen 曇無讖, 385–433) is entitled Sutra of the Compassionate Lotus Flower (Beihua jing 悲華經, T#157, 3: 167a–233c, see especially 185–186). For a detailed account on the recensions of this text and a bibliography see 126–127. and tells the story of a king who, with his 1011 sons, approaches Buddha Ratnagarbha (Baozang rulai 寶藏如來). This Buddha foretold that the king would become Buddha Amitābha in the future, while his first-born son, Bushun 不眴, “the one who never averts his eyes”, receives the name Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (Avalokiteśvara; Guanshiyin 觀世音), and is prophesized to become Amitābha’s successor, as Tathāgātā King of the Mountain of Meritorious Virtue Whose Light Illuminates Everything (Yi­qie guangming gongdeshan wang rulai 一切光明功德山王如來). The king’s second son will be known as Great Power Obtained (Mahāsthāmaprāpta; Dashizhi 大勢至), and will, in turn, succeed Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds after his nirvana. Bodhisattva Great Power Obtained will become Tathāgātā King of Precious Treasure Mountain Who Dwells Virtuously (Shanzhu zhenbaoshan wang rulai 善住珍寶山王如來).First noted by Wang Chang in his Assembled Edition of Epigraphica (preface 1805), 598, and discussed in 85. Recently see 36 and . The version of the story told in the Sutra of the Compassionate Lotus Flower seems to match perfectly the array of Buddha names on Mount Hongding. The name “Mountain King” (shanwang 山王) in the Tathāgātā names of Guanshiyin and Dashizhi, may even have promoted the carving of the Buddha names on this mountain cliff.

A similar story of the line of succession to Amitābha’s buddhahood is also found in the slightly later Sutra of Bodhisattva Perceiver of the World’s Sounds Receiving a Prophesy (Guanshiyin pusa shouji jing 觀世音菩薩授記經, T#371, 12: 356a–357a), translated by the Khotanese monk, Dharmodgata 曇無竭 (mid-fifth century), in 453. 36.

The third figure in the group formerly carved on Mount Culai, the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds, is here, in anticipation, also named a Buddha (觀世音佛). The veneration of the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds as a Buddha is attested by material and textual evidence from the fifth century onward. Kuramoto Shōtoku 倉本尚德 of the Ryūkoku University Research Center for Buddhist Cultures in Asia (Ryūkoku daigaku ajia bukkyō bunka kenkyū sentā 龍谷大学アジア仏教文化研究センター) has identified two sculptures of a Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds, dated to the years 532 and 541 respectively, as well as nine votive inscriptions with the name, Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (Guanshiyin fo 觀世音佛), the earliest from 495 and the latest from 575. . The Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds on Mount Culai is thus no isolated case.

In conclusion, the central theme of the group of the three Buddha names at Mount Culai seems to be the transmission of buddhahood into the future: In the same way that Maitreya will become a fully awakened Buddha in our own world, Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds will take Amitābha’s place in the Western Pure Land.

div.head: CLS 10
div.p: The name Buddha King of Great Emptiness (Da kong wang fo 大空王佛) is only preserved in rubbings on single sheets of paper. According to epigraphic sources, it was carved to the left of the three Buddha names CLS 9. 597–598; 4: juan 22, 481; 215. As this rubbing and those of CLS 9 are of the same height, 105 cm, 8: 17. the name Buddha King of Great Emptiness may have been placed near the other three Buddha names, and should also be considered a part of the group.

In this case, the name Buddha King of Great Emptiness would have followed after the name, Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds. A possible connection between these two Buddhas is provided by the apocryphal Sutra on the Ten Great Vows Taken by the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (Guanshiyin shi da yuan jing 觀世音十大願經).For this sutra see Mount Tao, Inscriptions p. . Here, the Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds receives the prophecy about his future buddhahood from a Buddha named “Buddha King of Emptiness Perceiver of the World’s Sounds 空王觀世音佛”.For the meaning of Buddha King of Great Emptiness see 82–84. This composite name is only known from this sutra. It establishes a close relation between the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds becoming a Buddha and the teachings of Emptiness.

div.head: CLS 1
div.p:
The celebrated passage of 98 characters from the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī
( Sapta­śatikā­prajñā­pāramitā-sūtra , Wen­shu­shi­li suo shuo mohe bore boluomi jing
文殊師利所説摩訶般若波羅蜜經) is carved on the Radiant Buddha Cliff.
For a discussion of this passage see Mount Jian (JS 6), p. , and Mount Shuiniu (SNS 2), p. , in the present volume.

The field of script is a horizontal rectangle in 14 columns, with 7 characters in each column. As a result, all 14 columns are of equal length. In all layouts of the passage carved elsewhere, the last column is not completely filled. Only the carving at the Cave of the Bewitching Fairy (Yaojing Dong 妖精洞) at Mount Yi (Yi 3) has columns of equal length. 75–83. However, there the characters are arranged in a vertical rectangle, in 7 columns with 14 characters each.

figure.head: Eastern half of Radiant Buddha Cliff with inscriptions CLS 2–4; photograph taken in 2007
映佛巖東半部徂徠山2號至4號刻銘。2007年拍攝

div.p: Another distinguishing feature of the different carvings of the 98 character passage in Shandong is the usage of the character wu, which may be written in the full form 無, or, as here, in the abbreviated form 无.

figure.head: Radiant Buddha Cliff seen from the southwest, with a large, even boulder in front; photograph taken in 2007
映佛巖西南面及前方平坦的巨石。2007年拍攝

div.head: CLS 5
div.p: The Seventeen Kinds of Emptiness on the boulder correspond to several passages in the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra ( Pañca­viṃśatisāhasrikā­prajñā­pāramitā ; Mohe bore boluomi jing 摩訶般若波羅蜜經; T#223). As this sutra is extensively quoted in the Treatise on the Great Perfection of Wisdom (* Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra ; Da zhidu lun 大智度論; T#1509), the relevant passages are found there, too. However, the carved heading claims to quote the Great Wisdom Sutra (Da bore jing 大般若經).A sutra with this precise title does not exist in the Buddhist canon.

The Seventeen Kinds of Emptiness are listed on the rock, one after the other, without further explanation. Doctrinally, they are a variation of what is commonly known as Eighteen Kinds of Emptiness (shiba kong 十八空).The set of the Eighteen Kinds of Emptiness, as found in Xuanzang’s 玄奘 (ca. 602–664) translation of the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra (Da bore boluomi jing 大般若波羅蜜多經, T#220), is translated and explained in 255–263. As defined in the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra,“The Buddha, knowing what Subhūti was thinking, told Subhūti: ‘The Six Perfections are the path of the bodhisattva-mahāsattva; the Thirty-seven Methods of Aid on the Path (Factors of Enlightenment) are the path of the bodhisattva-mahāsattva; the Eighteen Kinds of Emptiness are the path of the bodhisattva-mahāsattva... ’ 佛知須菩提心所念，告須菩提：六波羅蜜是菩薩摩訶薩道，三十七助道法是菩薩摩訶薩道，十八空是菩薩摩訶薩道……”(T#223, 8: 407b11–14). they are one more form of the bodhisattva path in Mahayana Buddhism, like the Six Perfections ( ṣaṭpāramitā ; liu boluomi 六波羅蜜)Text passages about the Six Perfections are carved in Shandong at Mount Hongding (HDS 2; 88–89; 103–107), on the Stele from Pingyin County (PY 1; 453–458), at Mount Tie (Tie 1, 106–109; 117–147), and at Mount Jian (JS 12, see Mount Jian; Inscriptions, p. , and the catalog entry on JS 12). and the Thirty-seven Factors of Enlightenment ( sapta­triṃśad­bodhi-pakṣikā­dharmāḥ ; sanshiqi daopin 三十七道品).For a discussion of the Thirty-seven Factors of Enlightenment, carved at Mount Tie (Tie 1), see 106–109; 117–147. Their successful practice leads to the realization of the Perfection of Wisdom, and thus, to buddhahood.

The Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra enumerates in scrolls 1, 3, 5, 22 and 24 seven instances of the Eighteen Kinds of Emptiness, with slight variations in their sequence. While items 1–11 as well as 16–18 are stable, items 12–15 may take different positions in these lists. The carving on the boulder differs in two instances: The Emptiness of Self-Attributes (zixiang kong 自相空) in the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra has been altered to the Emptiness of Self-dharmas (zifa kong
自法空) on the stone; the Emptiness of the Non-obtainable (bukede kong
不可得空) was not carved at all.

However, in scroll 22 of the sutra, the Emptiness of the Non-obtainable is omitted, too, resulting in only Seventeen Kinds of Emptiness.T#223, 8: 378c28–379a2. These match the carving on the stone exactly, with the exception that the positions of the Emptiness of Nature (xing kong
性空) and the Emptiness of Dharmas (zhufa kong
諸法空) have been reversed. Yet, in the manuscript of the Treatise on the Great Perfection of Wisdom, preserved in Ishiyama-dera 石山寺, the sequence of these two kinds of Emptiness is not reversed.T#1509, 25: 655, notes 2 and 3. This is the only text recension listing the Seventeen Kinds of Emptiness in the sequence given on the boulder.

Nevertheless, even this passage in the sutra does not offer an identical match to the stone version, because it inserts the character xing 行 (practicing) in front of some kinds of Emptiness.The full passage reads: “...practicing Emptiness of the Internal, practicing Emptiness of the External, practicing Emptiness of both Internal and External, practicing Emptiness of Emptiness, practicing Great Emptiness, Emptiness of the Primary Truth, Emptiness of the Conditioned, Emptiness of the Unconditioned, Emptiness of the Ultimate, Emptiness of the Beginningless, Emptiness of the Analyzed, Emptiness of Dharmas, Emptiness of Nature, Emptiness of Self-Attributes, Emptiness of Non-existent Dharmas, Emptiness of Existent Dharmas, Emptiness of Dharmas that are both Existent and Non-existent... ……行内空，行外空，行内外空，行空空，行大空、第一義空、有爲空、無爲空、畢竟空、無始空、散空、諸法空、性空、自相空、無法空、有法空、無法有法空……” (T#223, 8: 378c28–379a2). That character is not carved on the stone. The carved text corresponds closely, however, to a full list of Eighteen Kinds of Emptiness that is also found in scroll 22. It matches the sequence on the stone exactly, with just one additional Emptiness of the Non-obtainable.T#223, 8: 383a26–29. The corresponding passage in the Treatise on the Great Perfection of Wisdom is at T#1509, 25: 661b3–6. It reads in full: “Cultivating Emptiness of the Internal, Emptiness of the External, Emptiness of both Internal and External, Emptiness of Emptiness, Great Emptiness, Emptiness of the Primary Truth, Emptiness of the Conditioned, Emptiness of the Unconditioned, Emptiness of the Ultimate, Emptiness of the Beginningless, Emptiness of the Analyzed, Emptiness of Nature, Emptiness of Dharmas, Emptiness of Self-Attribute, Emptiness of the Non-obtainable, Emptiness of Non-existent Dharmas, Emptiness of Existent Dharmas, Emptiness of Dharmas that are both Existent and Non-existent; the refutation [of all these], this means cultivating prajñāpāramitā . 修内空、外空、内外空、空空、大空、第一義空、有爲空、無爲空、畢竟空、無始空、散空、性空、諸法空、自相空、不可得空、無法空、有法空、無法有法空壞，是修般若波羅蜜。”

The Eighteen Kinds of Emptiness were also carved at Mount Hongding, on the “Mahayana Sutra Stele” (HDS 5), but there, only a few characters of the text were completed. The planned text had to be reconstructed. In this case, a passage with the full list of all Eighteen Kinds of Emptiness taken from scroll 5 of the sutra provided the best match with the text on the rock.T#223, 8: 250b3–7, see 89 and 125–130.

This comparison of the various sets of Seventeen or Eighteen Kinds of Emptiness in the printed canon has revealed a host of minor differences among these sets and with the engraved version. Yet the variants are not significant or specific enough to allow the version on Mount Culai to be assigned to a particular school or period.

div.head: CLS 3
div.p: The name of the principal patron of all inscriptions at Mount Culai, Wang Zichun, appears on the upper half of the Radiant Buddha Cliff, immediately to the left of the five large characters reading “Perfection of Wisdom” (CLS 2). He is the Sutra Donor (jingzhu 經主) of the passage of 98 characters (CLS 1) carved below. In addition to being Magistrate of Liangfu County (Liangfu xianling 梁父縣令), Wang Zichun held the title of General Commanding the Troops (guanjun jiangjun 冠軍將軍), indicating that he had been involved in a military campaign.

div.head: CLS 4
div.p: This colophon contains the date, first year of the wuping era (570), and the names of donors. The first column names a certain Puxi 普憘, Universal Joy, who was either a monk or a layperson without any title. The third column refers to the “sangha together with (seng qi 僧齊) the large assembly (dazhong 大衆)” as donors. The name of a certain Huiyou 慧遊, who served as Overseer (weina 維那), is added in the last column.

div.head: CLS 6
div.p: This is the main colophon on the boulder close to Guanghua Monastery, following immediately after the carved passage on the Seventeen Kinds of Emptiness. Like the colophon CLS 3 at Radiant Buddha Cliff, it names Wang Zichun with his titles, General Commanding the Troops and County Magistrate of Liangfu. In addition, this colophon also lists the names of five of his sons as co-donors. The last name is probably that of a cleric, reading Sengzhen 僧真. Therefore, the inscription on the boulder was probably a family donation.

div.head: CLS 7
div.p: The name, Wang Shigui 王世貴, is awkwardly placed below column 6 of the passage on the Seventeen Kinds of Emptiness, and may be a later addition. This person seems to have been a member of Wang Zichun’s clan, but probably was not one of his children, as their names are already given in colophon CLS 6.

div.head: CLS 8
div.p: This colophon on the east side of the boulder records Rectifier Hu Bin and the date of the first year of the wuping era (570). The title, Rectifier (zhongzheng 中正), names a post assigning status ranks to local males eligible for government office. 189. As an official, Hu Bin is likely to have been acquainted with the County Magistrate, Wang Zichun.

The colophon may have been connected with the group of Buddha names preserved only in rubbings (CLS 9 and CLS 10), as several epigraphic sources say that it followed these names. See the Discussion to CLS 8, p. .

div.head: Carving Techniques
div.p: Three carving techniques were employed here:
